title: Universal Shuffle Asymptotics, Part III: Dominant-Block Quotient Geometry and Hybrid Gaussian--Compound-Poisson Limits in Finite-Alphabet Shuffle Privacy
comment: 36 pages. Part III of a trilogy; Part I: arXiv:2602.09029, Part II: arXiv:2603.10073. Numerical companion included on Zenodo (DOI:10.5281/zenodo.18978051)
summary: Part I of this series (arXiv:2602.09029) establishes a sharp Gaussian (LAN/GDP) limit theory for neighboring shuffle experiments in the fixed full-support regime. Part II (arXiv:2603.10073) identifies the first universality-breaking frontier: critical Poisson, Skellam, and multivariate compound-Poisson regimes. The present paper completes the finite-alphabet weak-limit theory by identifying the dominant-block quotient geometry that governs neighboring shuffle experiments. We treat dominant blocks of arbitrary finite size, allow overlap between the dominant output sets under the two neighboring hypotheses, and show that the limiting experiment decomposes according to this geometry: projecting onto the sum of the dominant tangent spaces yields a Gaussian factor, while quotienting by those same tangent spaces isolates a compound-Poisson jump field in the rare block. We also identify the regimes in which this quotient description determines the full privacy-curve, as well as the obstruction that appears when projected jump limits alone do not suffice. Two further sections sharpen the rate picture and the boundary interface: we show that the O(n^{-1/2}) rate for the full hybrid experiment is sharp in general, identify a compatibility condition restoring the O(n^{-1}) rate, and prove a boundary Berry--Esseen theorem giving O(c) Le Cam proximity between the critical Poisson-shift and Gaussian shift experiments as c tends to 0. Together with Parts I--II, this yields a three-regime universality picture and a precise finite-alphabet Levy--Khintchine layer for shuffle privacy.

AI.tldr: This paper completes the finite-alphabet weak-limit theory for neighboring shuffle experiments by identifying the dominant-block quotient geometry that governs these experiments, showing decomposition into Gaussian and compound-Poisson factors.
AI.motivation: To complete the finite-alphabet weak-limit theory for neighboring shuffle experiments, building on previous work that established Gaussian limits (Part I) and identified universality-breaking frontiers (Part II). The goal is to understand the geometric structure governing these experiments and provide a comprehensive three-regime universality picture.
AI.method: Identifies dominant-block quotient geometry that governs neighboring shuffle experiments, treating dominant blocks of arbitrary finite size and allowing overlap between dominant output sets. Shows decomposition of limiting experiment: projecting onto sum of dominant tangent spaces yields Gaussian factor, while quotienting by those tangent spaces isolates compound-Poisson jump field in rare block.
AI.result: Completes finite-alphabet weak-limit theory, shows O(n^{-1/2}) rate is sharp for full hybrid experiment, identifies compatibility condition restoring O(n^{-1}) rate, proves boundary Berry-Esseen theorem giving O(c) Le Cam proximity between critical Poisson-shift and Gaussian shift experiments as c→0. Together with Parts I-II, yields three-regime universality picture and precise finite-alphabet Levy-Khintchine layer for shuffle privacy.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a complete geometric understanding of neighboring shuffle experiments through dominant-block quotient decomposition, establishing sharp rate results and boundary behavior. This completes the three-regime universality picture for shuffle privacy with precise finite-alphabet Levy-Khintchine characterization.

title: Correlation Bounds and Markov Analysis for Ring-Oscillator TRNGs: A Joint Validation Framework
summary: True Random Number Generators (TRNGs) based on ring oscillators require rigorous statistical validation to ensure cryptographic quality. While the Mauduit-Sárközy $k$-th order correlation measure $C_k$ provides theoretical bounds on pseudorandomness, and Maurer's Universal Statistical Test offers empirical entropy assessment, no prior work has correlated these metrics. This paper presents the first joint validation framework linking Maurer's Z-score to off-peak 2nd-order correlation $C_2$. We also derive the mathematical relationship between the previous two measures and high-order Markov chain transition probabilities in counter-based TRNGs over oscillator sampling architectures. Our results are validated computationally using OpenTRNG implementations, and demonstrate that practical implementations achieve Schmidt's improved bound. The initial results suggest a strong positive correlation between Maurer Z-score and $C_2$. Therefore, the results suggest a unified metric for TRNG quality-assessment can be achieve as a combination of these metrics, simplifying the study of new designs.

AI.tldr: First joint validation framework linking Maurer's Z-score to off-peak 2nd-order correlation C₂ for ring oscillator TRNGs, deriving mathematical relationships with high-order Markov chains.
AI.motivation: TRNGs based on ring oscillators need rigorous statistical validation for cryptographic quality. While Mauduit-Sárközy k-th order correlation measure Cₖ provides theoretical bounds and Maurer's Universal Statistical Test offers empirical entropy assessment, no prior work has correlated these metrics.
AI.method: Developed a joint validation framework linking Maurer's Z-score to off-peak 2nd-order correlation C₂. Derived mathematical relationships between these measures and high-order Markov chain transition probabilities in counter-based TRNGs over oscillator sampling architectures.
AI.result: Validated computationally using OpenTRNG implementations, showing practical implementations achieve Schmidt's improved bound. Found strong positive correlation between Maurer Z-score and C₂.
AI.conclusion: A unified metric for TRNG quality-assessment can be achieved as a combination of these metrics, simplifying the study of new designs.

title: Multi-Agent SAC Enabled Beamforming Design for Joint Secret Key Generation and Data Transmission
comment: To appear in IEEE WCNC 2026 Workshop
summary: Physical layer key generation (PLKG) has emerged as a promising solution for achieving highly secured and low-latency key distribution, offering information-theoretic security that is inherently resilient to quantum attacks. However, simultaneously ensuring a high data transmission rate and a high secret key generation rate under eavesdropping attacks remains a major challenge. In time-division duplex (TDD) systems with multiple antennas, we derive closed-form expressions for both rates by modeling the legitimate channel as a time-correlated autoregressive (AR) process. This formulation leads to a highly nonconvex and time-coupled optimization problem, rendering traditional optimization methods ineffective. To address this issue, we propose a multi-agent soft actor-critic (SAC) framework equipped with a long short-term memory (LSTM) adversary prediction module to cope with the partial observability of the eavesdropper's mode. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves superior performance compared with other benchmark algorithms, while effectively balancing the trade-off between secret key generation rate and data transmission rate. The results also confirm the robustness of the proposed framework against intelligent eavesdropping and partial observation uncertainty.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a multi-agent SAC framework with LSTM adversary prediction to optimize secret key generation and data transmission rates in TDD systems under eavesdropping attacks.
AI.motivation: Physical layer key generation offers quantum-resistant security but faces challenges in simultaneously achieving high data transmission rates and high secret key generation rates under eavesdropping attacks in TDD systems with multiple antennas.
AI.method: The authors derive closed-form expressions for both rates by modeling the legitimate channel as a time-correlated AR process, then propose a multi-agent soft actor-critic (SAC) framework with LSTM adversary prediction module to handle partial observability of eavesdropper's mode and solve the nonconvex time-coupled optimization problem.
AI.result: Simulation results show the proposed approach achieves superior performance compared to benchmark algorithms, effectively balances the trade-off between secret key generation rate and data transmission rate, and demonstrates robustness against intelligent eavesdropping and partial observation uncertainty.
AI.conclusion: The multi-agent SAC framework with LSTM prediction successfully addresses the challenging optimization problem in PLKG systems, providing an effective solution for secure and efficient key distribution in TDD systems under eavesdropping threats.

title: On the Non-existence of Perfect Sequences with the Array Orthogonality Property
summary: For over three decades, the pursuit of perfect periodic autocorrelation sequences has been constrained by Mow's conjecture, which posits that no perfect sequence over an $n$-phase alphabet can exist with a length greater than $n^2$. While a proof across all conceivable sequence classes remains an open problem, this paper establishes bounds for a prominent class of constructions relying on the Array Orthogonality Property (AOP). We show that sequences generated by pure bivariate polynomial index functions cannot exceed the $n^2$ Frank-Heimiller bound due to algebraic periodicity. Furthermore, we extend this result to floored rational index functions, proving that attempts to geometrically expand the array dimensions inherently result in destructive fractional phase scattering. Neutralising this scattering strictly forces a collapse of the phase space, re-establishing the $n^2$ limit. Finally, we define the boundaries of these theorems, noting their fundamental reliance on commutative algebras, and contrast them with recent sequence constructions demonstrating the existence of unbounded perfect sequences over non-commutative unit quaternions.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes algebraic bounds on perfect periodic autocorrelation sequences, showing that sequences generated by bivariate polynomial or rational index functions cannot exceed the n² limit, while noting that unbounded perfect sequences exist over non-commutative algebras.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address Mow's conjecture about perfect periodic autocorrelation sequences, which has constrained sequence design for over three decades by suggesting no perfect sequence over an n-phase alphabet can exceed length n². The paper aims to establish rigorous bounds for important construction classes.
AI.method: The paper analyzes sequences generated by Array Orthogonality Property (AOP) constructions. It examines two classes: 1) pure bivariate polynomial index functions, showing they cannot exceed n² due to algebraic periodicity, and 2) floored rational index functions, proving geometric expansion attempts cause destructive fractional phase scattering that forces phase space collapse back to n².
AI.result: The paper proves that sequences generated by pure bivariate polynomial index functions are bounded by the n² Frank-Heimiller limit. For floored rational index functions, attempts to expand array dimensions cause fractional phase scattering that strictly forces phase space collapse, re-establishing the n² limit. The theorems fundamentally rely on commutative algebras.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes algebraic bounds for perfect periodic autocorrelation sequences in commutative algebras, showing the n² limit holds for important construction classes. However, it notes these bounds don't apply to non-commutative algebras, as recent constructions demonstrate unbounded perfect sequences exist over non-commutative unit quaternions.

title: Human Attribution of Causality to AI Across Agency, Misuse, and Misalignment
summary: AI-related incidents are becoming increasingly frequent and severe, ranging from safety failures to misuse by malicious actors. In such complex situations, identifying which elements caused an adverse outcome, the problem of cause selection, is a critical first step for establishing liability. This paper investigates folk perceptions of causal responsibility in causal chain structures when AI systems are involved in harmful outcomes. We conduct human experiments to examine judgments of causality, blame, foreseeability, and counterfactual reasoning. Our findings show that: (1) When AI agency was moderate (human sets the goal, AI determines the means) or high (AI sets the goal and the means), participants attributed greater causal responsibility to the AI. However, under low AI agency (where a human sets both a goal and means) participants assigned greater causal responsibility to the human despite their temporal distance from the outcome and despite both agents intended it, suggesting an effect of autonomy; (2) When we reversed roles between human and AI, participants consistently judged the human as more causal, even when both agents perform the same action; (3) The developer, despite being distant in the chain, was judged highly causal, reducing causal attributions to the human user but not to the AI; (4) Decomposing the AI into a large language model and an agentic component showed that the agentic part was judged as more causal in the chain. Overall, our research provides evidence on how people perceive the causal contribution of AI in both misuse and misalignment scenarios, and how these judgments interact with the roles of users and developers, key actors in assigning responsibility. These findings can inform the design of liability frameworks for AI-caused harms and shed light on how intuitive judgments shape social and policy debates surrounding real-world AI-related incidents.

AI.tldr: People attribute greater causal responsibility to AI when it has moderate or high agency, but blame humans more when AI has low agency. Human roles are consistently judged as more causal than AI even when performing identical actions. Developers are seen as highly causal despite distance in causal chains.
AI.motivation: As AI incidents increase in frequency and severity, understanding how people assign causal responsibility is crucial for establishing liability frameworks. The paper aims to investigate folk perceptions of causal responsibility in AI-involved harmful outcomes, particularly examining how judgments differ based on AI agency levels and agent roles.
AI.method: Conducted human experiments examining judgments of causality, blame, foreseeability, and counterfactual reasoning in various AI-involved scenarios. Tested different levels of AI agency (low, moderate, high), role reversals between humans and AI, developer involvement, and decomposition of AI into LLM and agentic components.
AI.result: 1) Moderate/high AI agency → greater causal responsibility to AI; low AI agency → greater responsibility to humans despite temporal distance. 2) Humans consistently judged as more causal than AI even with identical actions. 3) Developers judged highly causal, reducing attributions to human users but not AI. 4) Agentic AI components judged more causal than LLM components.
AI.conclusion: People's causal judgments about AI-related harms are influenced by perceived autonomy and agency levels, with humans consistently seen as more responsible even in comparable scenarios. These folk perceptions have important implications for liability frameworks and policy debates surrounding real-world AI incidents.

title: Self-Supervised Multi-Stage Domain Unlearning for White-Matter Lesion Segmentation
summary: Inter-scanner variability of magnetic resonance imaging has an adverse impact on the diagnostic and prognostic quality of the scans and necessitates the development of models robust to domain shift inflicted by the unseen scanner data. Review of recent advances in domain adaptation showed that efficacy of strategies involving modifications or constraints on the latent space appears to be contingent upon the level and/or depth of supervision during model training. In this paper, we therefore propose an unsupervised domain adaptation technique based on self-supervised multi-stage unlearning (SSMSU). Building upon the state-of-the-art segmentation framework nnU-Net, we employ deep supervision at deep encoder stages using domain classifier unlearning, applied sequentially across the deep stages to suppress domain-related latent features. Following self-configurable approach of the nnU-Net, the auxiliary feedback loop implements a self-supervised backpropagation schedule for the unlearning process, since continuous unlearning was found to have a detrimental effect on the main segmentation task. Experiments were carried out on four public datasets for benchmarking white-matter lesion segmentation methods. Five benchmark models and/or strategies, covering passive to active unsupervised domain adaptation, were tested. In comparison, the SSMSU demonstrated the advantage of unlearning by enhancing lesion sensitivity and limiting false detections, which resulted in higher overall segmentation quality in terms of segmentation overlap and relative lesion volume error. The proposed model inputs only the FLAIR modality, which simplifies preprocessing pipelines, eliminates the need for inter-modality registration errors and harmonization, which can introduce variability. Source code is available on https://github.com/Pubec/nnunetv2-unlearning.

AI.tldr: Proposes SSMSU - a self-supervised multi-stage unlearning method for unsupervised domain adaptation in MRI lesion segmentation, built on nnU-Net with deep supervision and domain classifier unlearning to handle scanner variability.
AI.motivation: Inter-scanner MRI variability causes domain shift problems that degrade diagnostic/prognostic quality. Need robust models that can adapt to unseen scanner data without supervision. Existing domain adaptation methods show inconsistent effectiveness depending on supervision levels.
AI.method: SSMSU: Self-supervised multi-stage unlearning built on nnU-Net framework. Uses deep supervision at encoder stages with domain classifier unlearning applied sequentially across deep stages to suppress domain-related features. Implements self-supervised backpropagation schedule for unlearning to avoid harming main segmentation task. Only requires FLAIR modality input.
AI.result: Tested on four public white-matter lesion segmentation datasets against five benchmark UDA methods. SSMSU showed advantages in enhancing lesion sensitivity, limiting false detections, achieving higher segmentation overlap and lower relative lesion volume error compared to other methods.
AI.conclusion: SSMSU effectively addresses MRI scanner variability through unsupervised domain adaptation using multi-stage unlearning. The single-modality (FLAIR) approach simplifies preprocessing and avoids registration/harmonization issues. Method demonstrates practical value for clinical applications where scanner variability is problematic.

title: Task-Oriented Wireless Transmission of 3D Point Clouds: Geometric Versus Semantic Robustness
summary: Wireless transmission of high-dimensional 3D point clouds (PCs) is increasingly required in industrial collaborative robotics systems. Conventional compression methods prioritize geometric fidelity, although many practical applications ultimately depend on reliable task-level inference rather than exact coordinate reconstruction. In this paper, we propose an end-to-end semantic communication framework for wireless 3D PC transmission and conduct a systematic study of the relationship between geometric reconstruction fidelity and semantic robustness under channel impairments. The proposed architecture jointly supports geometric recovery and object classification from a shared transmitted representation, enabling direct comparison between coordinate-level and task-level sensitivity to noise. Experimental evaluation on a real industrial dataset reveals a pronounced asymmetry: semantic inference remains stable across a broad signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) range even when geometric reconstruction quality degrades significantly. These results demonstrate that reliable task execution does not require high-fidelity geometric recovery and provide design insights for task-oriented wireless perception systems in bandwidth- and power-constrained industrial environments.

AI.tldr: Semantic communication for 3D point clouds prioritizes task-level inference over geometric fidelity, showing semantic robustness persists even when geometric reconstruction degrades under channel noise.
AI.motivation: Industrial robotics need wireless 3D point cloud transmission, but conventional compression focuses on geometric fidelity while practical applications rely on task-level inference reliability rather than exact coordinate reconstruction.
AI.method: Proposed end-to-end semantic communication framework for wireless 3D PC transmission that jointly supports geometric recovery and object classification from shared transmitted representation, enabling comparison between coordinate-level and task-level noise sensitivity.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation reveals semantic inference remains stable across broad SNR range even when geometric reconstruction quality degrades significantly, showing pronounced asymmetry between geometric and semantic robustness.
AI.conclusion: Reliable task execution doesn't require high-fidelity geometric recovery, providing design insights for task-oriented wireless perception systems in bandwidth- and power-constrained industrial environments.

title: KazakhOCR: A Synthetic Benchmark for Evaluating Multimodal Models in Low-Resource Kazakh Script OCR
summary: Kazakh is a Turkic language using the Arabic, Cyrillic, and Latin scripts, making it unique in terms of optical character recognition (OCR). Work on OCR for low-resource Kazakh scripts is very scarce, and no OCR benchmarks or images exist for the Arabic and Latin scripts. We construct a synthetic OCR dataset of 7,219 images for all three scripts with font, color, and noise variations to imitate real OCR tasks. We evaluated three multimodal large language models (MLLMs) on a subset of the benchmark for OCR and language identification: Gemma-3-12B-it, Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct, and Llama-3.2-11B-Vision-Instruct. All models are unsuccessful with Latin and Arabic script OCR, and fail to recognize the Arabic script as Kazakh text, misclassifying it as Arabic, Farsi, and Kurdish. We further compare MLLMs with a classical OCR baseline and find that while traditional OCR has lower character error rates, MLLMs fail to match this performance. These findings show significant gaps in current MLLM capabilities to process low-resource Abjad-based scripts and demonstrate the need for inclusive models and benchmarks supporting low-resource scripts and languages.

AI.tldr: MLLMs fail at OCR for low-resource Kazakh scripts (Arabic and Latin), performing worse than traditional OCR and misidentifying Arabic script Kazakh as other languages.
AI.motivation: Kazakh uses three scripts (Arabic, Cyrillic, Latin) but lacks OCR benchmarks for Arabic and Latin scripts, creating a gap for low-resource language OCR evaluation.
AI.method: Created synthetic OCR dataset of 7,219 images with font/color/noise variations for all three scripts. Evaluated three MLLMs (Gemma-3-12B-it, Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct, Llama-3.2-11B-Vision-Instruct) and compared with traditional OCR baseline.
AI.result: All MLLMs failed on Latin and Arabic script OCR, misclassifying Arabic script Kazakh as Arabic, Farsi, Kurdish. Traditional OCR had lower character error rates than MLLMs.
AI.conclusion: Significant gaps exist in MLLM capabilities for low-resource Abjad-based scripts, highlighting need for more inclusive models and benchmarks supporting diverse scripts and languages.

title: Translational Gaps in Graph Transformers for Longitudinal EHR Prediction: A Critical Appraisal of GT-BEHRT
comment: A critical review of graph transformer models for longitudinal electronic health records, discussing evaluation practices, calibration, fairness, and clinical relevance. 5 pages
summary: Transformer-based models have improved predictive modeling on longitudinal electronic health records through large-scale self-supervised pretraining. However, most EHR transformer architectures treat each clinical encounter as an unordered collection of codes, which limits their ability to capture meaningful relationships within a visit. Graph-transformer approaches aim to address this limitation by modeling visit-level structure while retaining the ability to learn long-term temporal patterns. This paper provides a critical review of GT-BEHRT, a graph-transformer architecture evaluated on MIMIC-IV intensive care outcomes and heart failure prediction in the All of Us Research Program. We examine whether the reported performance gains reflect genuine architectural benefits and whether the evaluation methodology supports claims of robustness and clinical relevance. We analyze GT-BEHRT across seven dimensions relevant to modern machine learning systems, including representation design, pretraining strategy, cohort construction transparency, evaluation beyond discrimination, fairness assessment, reproducibility, and deployment feasibility. GT-BEHRT reports strong discrimination for heart failure prediction within 365 days, with AUROC 94.37 +/- 0.20, AUPRC 73.96 +/- 0.83, and F1 64.70 +/- 0.85. Despite these results, we identify several important gaps, including the lack of calibration analysis, incomplete fairness evaluation, sensitivity to cohort selection, limited analysis across phenotypes and prediction horizons, and limited discussion of practical deployment considerations. Overall, GT-BEHRT represents a meaningful architectural advance in EHR representation learning, but more rigorous evaluation focused on calibration, fairness, and deployment is needed before such models can reliably support clinical decision-making.

AI.tldr: Critical review of GT-BEHRT graph-transformer architecture for EHR analysis finds meaningful architectural advance but identifies gaps in evaluation methodology needed for clinical deployment.
AI.motivation: Most EHR transformer architectures treat clinical encounters as unordered collections of codes, limiting their ability to capture meaningful relationships within visits. Graph-transformer approaches aim to model visit-level structure while retaining long-term temporal pattern learning.
AI.method: Critical review of GT-BEHRT across seven dimensions: representation design, pretraining strategy, cohort construction transparency, evaluation beyond discrimination, fairness assessment, reproducibility, and deployment feasibility. Evaluated on MIMIC-IV intensive care outcomes and heart failure prediction in All of Us Research Program.
AI.result: GT-BEHRT reports strong discrimination for heart failure prediction (AUROC 94.37 +/- 0.20, AUPRC 73.96 +/- 0.83, F1 64.70 +/- 0.85). However, review identifies gaps: lack of calibration analysis, incomplete fairness evaluation, sensitivity to cohort selection, limited analysis across phenotypes/prediction horizons, and limited deployment discussion.
AI.conclusion: GT-BEHRT represents a meaningful architectural advance in EHR representation learning, but more rigorous evaluation focused on calibration, fairness, and deployment is needed before such models can reliably support clinical decision-making.

title: Rationale Behind Human-Led Autonomous Truck Platooning
summary: Autonomous trucking has progressed rapidly in recent years, transitioning from early demonstrations to OEM-integrated commercial deployments. However, fully driverless freight operations across heterogeneous climates, infrastructure conditions, and regulatory environments remain technically and socially challenging. This paper presents a systematic rationale for human-led autonomous truck platooning as a pragmatic intermediate pathway. First, we analyze 53 major truck accidents across North America (2021-2026) and show that human-related factors remain the dominant contributors to severe crashes, highlighting both the need for advanced assistance/automated driving systems and the complexity of real-world driving environments. Second, we review recent industry developments and identify persistent limitations in long-tail edge cases, winter operations, remote-region logistics, and large-scale safety validation. Based on these findings, we argue that a human-in-the-loop (HiL) platooning architecture offers layered redundancy, adaptive judgment in uncertain conditions, and a scalable validation framework. Furthermore, the dual-use capability of follower vehicles enables an evolutionary transition from coordinated platooning to independent autonomous operation. Rather than representing a compromise, human-led platooning provides a technically grounded and societally aligned bridge toward large-scale autonomous freight deployment.

AI.tldr: Human-led autonomous truck platooning as a pragmatic intermediate pathway to full autonomy, addressing safety gaps and enabling evolutionary transition.
AI.motivation: Fully driverless freight operations face technical and social challenges across diverse conditions. Human-related factors remain dominant in severe truck accidents, highlighting need for advanced systems while acknowledging real-world complexity.
AI.method: 1) Analysis of 53 major truck accidents (2021-2026) to identify human-related factors; 2) Review of industry developments to identify persistent limitations; 3) Proposal of human-in-the-loop platooning architecture with layered redundancy.
AI.result: Human-related factors are dominant contributors to severe crashes. Industry faces limitations in edge cases, winter operations, remote logistics, and safety validation. Human-led platooning offers practical solution with adaptive judgment and scalable validation.
AI.conclusion: Human-led autonomous truck platooning is not a compromise but a technically grounded, societally aligned bridge toward large-scale autonomous freight deployment, providing evolutionary transition capability.

title: Shared Sky, Shared Spectrum: Coordinated Satellite-5G Networks for Low-Altitude Economy
summary: Driven by both technological development and practical demands, the low-altitude economy relying on low-altitude aircrafts (LAAs) is booming. However, neither satellites nor terrestrial fifth-generation (5G) networks alone can effectively satisfy the communication requirements for ubiquitous lowaltitude coverage. While full integration of satellites and 5G networks offers theoretical benefits, the associated overhead and complexity pose significant challenges for rapid deployment. As a more economical and immediately viable alternative, this paper investigates partially-integrated networks where satellites and 5G systems operate with coarse synchronization yet achieve coordinated spectrum sharing, pooling their capabilities to jointly serve LAAs. Leveraging the inherent position-awareness of LAAs, we propose a framework for joint time-frequency spectrum sharing with an adaptive synchronization time scale, where only large-scale channel state information (CSI) is required. To avoid solving the NP-hard optimization problem directly, link-feature-aided clustering is employed following a divide-andconquer strategy. The proposed framework achieves substantial performance gains with low overhead and complexity, enabling swift advancement of low-altitude applications while paving the way for future integrated satellite-terrestrial network evolution.

AI.tldr: Proposes a partially-integrated satellite-5G network with coordinated spectrum sharing to serve low-altitude aircrafts, using position-aware adaptive synchronization and link-feature clustering for efficient low-overhead communication.
AI.motivation: Low-altitude economy is growing but neither satellites nor terrestrial 5G networks alone can provide effective ubiquitous coverage for low-altitude aircrafts. Full satellite-5G integration has high overhead and complexity, so a more practical partial integration approach is needed.
AI.method: Proposes a partially-integrated network where satellites and 5G systems operate with coarse synchronization but achieve coordinated spectrum sharing. Uses position-awareness of LAAs for joint time-frequency spectrum sharing with adaptive synchronization time scale, requiring only large-scale CSI. Employs link-feature-aided clustering as a divide-and-conquer strategy to avoid solving NP-hard optimization directly.
AI.result: The proposed framework achieves substantial performance gains with low overhead and complexity, enabling swift advancement of low-altitude applications while paving the way for future integrated satellite-terrestrial network evolution.
AI.conclusion: Partially-integrated satellite-5G networks with coordinated spectrum sharing offer an economical and immediately viable alternative to full integration, effectively serving low-altitude aircraft communication needs while maintaining practical deployment feasibility.

title: A Dual-Path Generative Framework for Zero-Day Fraud Detection in Banking Systems
summary: High-frequency banking environments face a critical trade-off between low-latency fraud detection and the regulatory explainability demanded by GDPR. Traditional rule-based and discriminative models struggle with "zero-day" attacks due to extreme class imbalance and the lack of historical precedents. This paper proposes a Dual-Path Generative Framework that decouples real-time anomaly detection from offline adversarial training. The architecture employs a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) to establish a legitimate transaction manifold based on reconstruction error, ensuring <50ms inference latency. In parallel, an asynchronous Wasserstein GAN with Gradient Penalty (WGAN-GP) synthesizes high-entropy fraudulent scenarios to stress-test the detection boundaries. Crucially, to address the non-differentiability of discrete banking data (e.g., Merchant Category Codes), we integrate a Gumbel-Softmax estimator. Furthermore, we introduce a trigger-based explainability mechanism where SHAP (Shapley Additive Explanations) is activated only for high-uncertainty transactions, reconciling the computational cost of XAI with real-time throughput requirements.

AI.tldr: Dual-Path Generative Framework for banking fraud detection: VAE for real-time anomaly detection (<50ms) + WGAN-GP for offline adversarial training, with Gumbel-Softmax for discrete data and trigger-based SHAP for explainability.
AI.motivation: High-frequency banking needs low-latency fraud detection while meeting GDPR explainability requirements. Traditional methods fail with "zero-day" attacks due to class imbalance and lack of historical precedents.
AI.method: Dual-Path Generative Framework: 1) VAE for real-time anomaly detection using reconstruction error, 2) Asynchronous WGAN-GP synthesizes fraudulent scenarios for stress-testing, 3) Gumbel-Softmax handles discrete banking data, 4) Trigger-based SHAP activation for high-uncertainty transactions.
AI.result: Achieves <50ms inference latency for real-time detection while maintaining regulatory explainability through selective SHAP activation for high-uncertainty cases.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully reconciles low-latency fraud detection with GDPR-compliant explainability by decoupling real-time operations from offline adversarial training, addressing both technical and regulatory challenges in high-frequency banking.

title: Projection Guided Personalized Federated Learning for Low Dose CT Denoising
summary: Low-dose CT (LDCT) reduces radiation exposure but introduces protocol-dependent noise and artifacts that vary across institutions. While federated learning enables collaborative training without centralizing patient data, existing methods personalize in image space, making it difficult to separate scanner noise from patient anatomy. We propose ProFed (Projection Guided Personalized Federated Learning), a framework that complements the image space approach by performing dual-level personalization in the projection space, where noise originates during CT measurements before reconstruction combines protocol and anatomy effects. ProFed introduces: (i) anatomy-aware and protocol-aware networks that personalize CT reconstruction to patient and scanner-specific features, (ii) multi-constraint projection losses that enforce consistency with CT measurements, and (iii) uncertainty-guided selective aggregation that weights clients by prediction confidence. Extensive experiments on the Mayo Clinic 2016 dataset demonstrate that ProFed achieves 42.56 dB PSNR with CNN backbones and 44.83 dB with Transformers, outperforming 11 federated learning baselines, including the physics-informed SCAN-PhysFed by +1.42 dB.

AI.tldr: ProFed is a projection-guided personalized federated learning framework for low-dose CT reconstruction that performs dual-level personalization in projection space to separate scanner noise from patient anatomy, outperforming 11 federated learning baselines.
AI.motivation: Low-dose CT reduces radiation exposure but introduces protocol-dependent noise and artifacts that vary across institutions. Existing federated learning methods personalize in image space, making it difficult to separate scanner noise from patient anatomy.
AI.method: ProFed performs dual-level personalization in projection space where noise originates. It introduces: (1) anatomy-aware and protocol-aware networks for CT reconstruction, (2) multi-constraint projection losses for consistency with CT measurements, and (3) uncertainty-guided selective aggregation that weights clients by prediction confidence.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on Mayo Clinic 2016 dataset show ProFed achieves 42.56 dB PSNR with CNN backbones and 44.83 dB with Transformers, outperforming 11 federated learning baselines including physics-informed SCAN-PhysFed by +1.42 dB.
AI.conclusion: ProFed effectively addresses the limitations of image-space personalization by performing dual-level personalization in projection space, enabling better separation of scanner noise from patient anatomy in federated low-dose CT reconstruction.

title: Extended Target Sensing in MIMO-OFDM ISAC Systems: Modeling, Optimization and Estimation
summary: This paper develops a comprehensive target modeling, beamforming optimization, and parameter estimation framework for extended-target sensing in wideband MIMO-OFDM integrated sensing and communication systems. We propose a parametric scattering model (PSM) that decouples target geometry from electromagnetic scattering characteristics, requiring only six nonlinear geometric parameters and linear radar cross-section (RCS) coefficients. Based on this compact structure, we derive a hybrid Bayesian Cramér-Rao bound (CRB) for joint estimation of azimuth, elevation, and range-related parameters. To handle inherent range ambiguities due to OFDM signaling, we analyze the range ambiguity function and introduce range sidelobe suppression constraints around the true range. Based on these constraints, we formulate an ambiguity-aware transmit beamforming design that minimizes a weighted geometric CRB subject to per-user signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR) requirements and a total power budget. As benchmarks, we extend two other common models to the same wideband MIMO-OFDM scenario. We also derive maximum a posteriori estimators and a computational complexity analysis for all three models. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed PSM-based approach achieves improved target localization with significantly reduced runtime for beamforming optimization and parameter estimation, while consistently satisfying communication SINR requirements.

AI.tldr: Proposes parametric scattering model for extended-target sensing in wideband MIMO-OFDM ISAC systems with compact parameterization, ambiguity-aware beamforming, and improved localization performance.
AI.motivation: Need for efficient extended-target sensing in integrated sensing and communication systems that handles range ambiguities inherent in OFDM signaling while maintaining communication quality of service.
AI.method: Develops parametric scattering model decoupling geometry from scattering, derives hybrid Bayesian CRB, analyzes range ambiguity function, designs ambiguity-aware transmit beamforming with SINR constraints, and implements MAP estimators.
AI.result: PSM-based approach achieves improved target localization with significantly reduced runtime for beamforming optimization and parameter estimation while consistently satisfying communication SINR requirements.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides an efficient solution for extended-target sensing in wideband MIMO-OFDM ISAC systems with compact modeling, ambiguity handling, and computational advantages over benchmark approaches.

title: Gloss-Free Sign Language Translation: An Unbiased Evaluation of Progress in the Field
comment: This is a preprint of an article published in Computer Vision and Image Understanding (CVIU)
summary:
Sign Language Translation (SLT) aims to automatically convert visual sign language videos into spoken language text and vice versa.
  While recent years have seen rapid progress, the true sources of performance improvements often remain unclear. Do reported performance gains come from methodological novelty, or from the choice of a different backbone, training optimizations, hyperparameter tuning, or even differences in the calculation of evaluation metrics? This paper presents a comprehensive study of recent gloss-free SLT models by re-implementing key contributions in a unified codebase. We ensure fair comparison by standardizing preprocessing, video encoders, and training setups across all methods. Our analysis shows that many of the performance gains reported in the literature often diminish when models are evaluated under consistent conditions, suggesting that implementation details and evaluation setups play a significant role in determining results. We make the codebase publicly available here (https://github.com/ozgemercanoglu/sltbaselines) to support transparency and reproducibility in SLT research.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive study of gloss-free Sign Language Translation models shows that many reported performance gains diminish under standardized evaluation, revealing that implementation details and evaluation setups significantly impact results.
AI.motivation: To address the unclear sources of performance improvements in Sign Language Translation research, where it's difficult to determine if gains come from methodological novelty or from implementation details like backbone choice, training optimizations, hyperparameter tuning, or evaluation metric differences.
AI.method: Re-implemented key contributions of recent gloss-free SLT models in a unified codebase with standardized preprocessing, video encoders, and training setups across all methods to ensure fair comparison.
AI.result: Many performance gains reported in literature diminish when models are evaluated under consistent conditions, suggesting that implementation details and evaluation setups play a significant role in determining results rather than methodological novelty alone.
AI.conclusion: The study highlights the importance of standardized evaluation and transparent implementation in SLT research, and provides a publicly available codebase to support reproducibility and fair comparison in the field.

title: RFX-Fuse: Breiman and Cutler's Unified ML Engine + Native Explainable Similarity
summary:
Breiman and Cutler's original Random Forest was designed as a unified ML engine -- not merely an ensemble predictor. Their implementation included classification, regression, unsupervised learning, proximity-based similarity, outlier
  detection, missing value imputation, and visualization -- capabilities that modern libraries like scikit-learn never implemented. RFX-Fuse (Random Forests X [X=compression] -- Forest Unified Learning and Similarity Engine) delivers
  Breiman and Cutler's complete vision with native GPU/CPU support. Modern ML pipelines require 5+ separate tools -- XGBoost for prediction, FAISS for similarity, SHAP for explanations, Isolation Forest for outliers, custom code for
  importance. RFX-Fuse provides a 1 to 2 model object alternative -- a single set of trees grown once. Novel Contributions: (1) Proximity Importance -- native explainable similarity: proximity measures that samples are similar;
  proximity importance explains why. (2) Dataset-specific imputation validation for general tabular data -- ranking imputation methods by how real the imputed data looks, without ground truth labels.

AI.tldr: RFX-Fuse revives Breiman & Cutler's original Random Forest vision as a unified ML engine with GPU/CPU support, replacing 5+ separate tools with one model object.
AI.motivation: Modern ML pipelines require multiple separate tools for different tasks (prediction, similarity, explanations, outlier detection, importance), while Breiman & Cutler's original Random Forest was designed as a unified engine with capabilities that modern libraries never implemented.
AI.method: RFX-Fuse delivers the complete Random Forest vision with native GPU/CPU support using a single set of trees grown once. It introduces Proximity Importance for explainable similarity and dataset-specific imputation validation for tabular data.
AI.result: Provides a 1-2 model object alternative to 5+ separate tools, enabling classification, regression, unsupervised learning, proximity-based similarity, outlier detection, missing value imputation, and visualization in a unified framework.
AI.conclusion: RFX-Fuse restores the original comprehensive Random Forest vision as a unified ML engine, reducing tool fragmentation while adding novel capabilities for explainable similarity and imputation validation.

title: MRPoS: Mixed Reality-Based Robot Navigation Interface Using Spatial Pointing and Speech with Large Language Model
summary: Recent advancements have made robot navigation more intuitive by transitioning from traditional 2D displays to spatially aware Mixed Reality (MR) systems. However, current MR interfaces often rely on manual "air tap" gestures for goal placement, which can be repetitive and physically demanding, especially for beginners. This paper proposes the Mixed Reality-Based Robot Navigation Interface using Spatial Pointing and Speech (MRPoS). This novel framework replaces complex hand gestures with a natural, multimodal interface combining spatial pointing with Large Language Model (LLM)-based speech interaction. By leveraging both information, the system translates verbal intent into navigation goals visualized by MR technology. Comprehensive experiments comparing MRPoS against conventional gesture-based systems demonstrate that our approach significantly reduces task completion time and workload, providing a more accessible and efficient interface. For additional material, please check: https://mertcookimg.github.io/mrpos

AI.tldr: MRPoS: A novel mixed reality robot navigation interface that replaces complex hand gestures with natural spatial pointing and LLM-based speech interaction, reducing task time and workload.
AI.motivation: Current MR interfaces for robot navigation rely on repetitive "air tap" gestures that are physically demanding, especially for beginners. There's a need for more natural and accessible interaction methods.
AI.method: Proposes MRPoS framework that combines spatial pointing with Large Language Model-based speech interaction. The system translates verbal intent into navigation goals visualized by MR technology, replacing complex hand gestures.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments show MRPoS significantly reduces task completion time and workload compared to conventional gesture-based systems, providing a more accessible and efficient interface.
AI.conclusion: MRPoS offers a natural multimodal interface that improves robot navigation in mixed reality by combining spatial pointing with speech interaction, making the system more intuitive and less physically demanding.

title: Identification for ISI Gaussian Channels
summary: We establish non-asymptotic lower and upper bounds for the identification capacity of discrete-time Gaussian channels subject to inter-symbol interference (ISI), a canonical model in wireless communication. Our analysis accounts for deterministic encoders under peak power constraint. A principal finding is that, even when the number of ISI taps scales sub-linearly with the codeword length, \(n\), i.e., \(\sim n^κ\) with \(κ\in [0,1/2),\) the number of messages that can be reliably identified grows super-exponentially in \(n\), i.e., \(\sim 2^{(n \log n)R}\), where \(R\) denotes the coding rate.

AI.tldr: Non-asymptotic bounds for identification capacity of Gaussian ISI channels show super-exponential message growth even with sub-linear ISI tap scaling.
AI.motivation: To characterize the identification capacity of Gaussian channels with inter-symbol interference (ISI), a fundamental wireless communication model, under practical constraints like deterministic encoders and peak power limitations.
AI.method: Establish non-asymptotic lower and upper bounds for identification capacity, analyzing deterministic encoders under peak power constraint, considering ISI taps scaling sub-linearly with codeword length.
AI.result: Even when ISI taps scale sub-linearly with n (∼n^κ with κ∈[0,1/2)), the number of reliably identifiable messages grows super-exponentially as ∼2^{(n log n)R}, where R is the coding rate.
AI.conclusion: Gaussian ISI channels support super-exponential message identification growth despite sub-linear ISI tap scaling, providing fundamental limits for wireless communication systems.

title: Benchmarking Zero-Shot Reasoning Approaches for Error Detection in Solidity Smart Contracts
summary: Smart contracts play a central role in blockchain systems by encoding financial and operational logic. Still, their susceptibility to subtle security flaws poses significant risks of financial loss and erosion of trust. LLMs create new opportunities for automating vulnerability detection, yet the effectiveness of different prompting strategies and model choices in real-world contexts remains uncertain. This paper evaluates state-of-the-art LLMs on Solidity smart contract analysis using a balanced dataset of 400 contracts under two tasks: (i) Error Detection, where the model performs binary classification to decide whether a contract is vulnerable, and (ii) Error Classification, where the model must assign the predicted issue to a specific vulnerability category. Models are evaluated using zero-shot prompting strategies, including zero-shot, zero-shot Chain-of-Thought (CoT), and zero-shot Tree-of-Thought (ToT). In the Error Detection task, CoT and ToT substantially increase recall (often approaching $\approx 95$--$99\%$), but typically reduce precision, indicating a more sensitive decision regime with more false positives. In the Error Classification task, Claude 3 Opus attains the best Weighted F1-score (90.8) under the ToT prompt, followed closely by its CoT.

AI.tldr: LLMs evaluated for smart contract vulnerability detection using zero-shot prompting strategies on 400 Solidity contracts, showing CoT and ToT improve recall but reduce precision.
AI.motivation: Smart contracts are critical but vulnerable to security flaws causing financial losses, and LLMs offer automation potential but their effectiveness with different prompting strategies in real-world contexts is uncertain.
AI.method: Evaluated state-of-the-art LLMs on 400 Solidity contracts using zero-shot prompting strategies (zero-shot, zero-shot Chain-of-Thought, zero-shot Tree-of-Thought) for two tasks: Error Detection (binary classification) and Error Classification (specific vulnerability category assignment).
AI.result: For Error Detection, CoT and ToT substantially increase recall (≈95-99%) but reduce precision, indicating more sensitive decision regime with more false positives. For Error Classification, Claude 3 Opus achieved best Weighted F1-score (90.8) under ToT prompt, closely followed by its CoT performance.
AI.conclusion: Prompting strategies significantly impact LLM performance in smart contract analysis, with CoT and ToT improving vulnerability detection sensitivity at the cost of precision, and Claude 3 Opus demonstrating strong classification capabilities.

title: Bayesian Uncertainty-Aware MRI Reconstruction
summary: We propose a novel framework for joint magnetic resonance image reconstruction and uncertainty quantification using under-sampled k-space measurements. The problem is formulated as a Bayesian linear inverse problem, where prior distributions are assigned to the unknown model parameters. Specifically, we assume the target image is sparse in its spatial gradient and impose a total variation prior model. A Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) method, based on a split-and-augmented Gibbs sampler, is then used to sample from the resulting joint posterior distribution of the unknown parameters. Experiments conducted using single- and multi-coil datasets demonstrate the superior performance of the proposed framework over optimisation-based compressed sensing algorithms. Additionally, our framework effectively quantifies uncertainty, showing strong correlation with error maps computed from reconstructed and ground-truth images.

AI.tldr: A Bayesian framework for MRI reconstruction with uncertainty quantification using MCMC sampling and total variation priors.
AI.motivation: To address the need for both accurate MRI reconstruction from under-sampled k-space data and reliable uncertainty quantification, which is lacking in traditional optimization-based compressed sensing methods.
AI.method: Formulates MRI reconstruction as a Bayesian linear inverse problem with total variation prior for sparsity in spatial gradients. Uses MCMC with split-and-augmented Gibbs sampler to sample from joint posterior distribution.
AI.result: Superior performance over optimization-based compressed sensing algorithms on single- and multi-coil datasets, with effective uncertainty quantification showing strong correlation with actual error maps.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Bayesian framework successfully combines accurate MRI reconstruction with meaningful uncertainty quantification, addressing limitations of traditional compressed sensing approaches.

title: Expressivity of Programmable-Metasurface-Based Physical Neural Networks: Encoding Non-Linearity, Structural Non-Linearity, and Depth
summary: Wave-based signal processing conventionally encodes input data into the input wavefront, making it challenging to implement non-linear operations. Programmable wave systems enable an alternative approach: encoding the input data into the scattering properties of tunable components. With such structural input encoding, two potentially non-linear mappings are involved: first, from the input data to the tunable components' scattering characteristics, and, second, from these scattering characteristics to the output wavefront. In this paper, we systematically examine the expressivity of a wave-based physical neural network (WPNN) with structural input encoding. Our analysis is based on a physics-consistent multiport-network model of a compact D-band rich-scattering cavity parametrized by a 100-element programmable metasurface. We separately control encoding non-linearity, structural non-linearity, and network depth in order to examine their interplay, considering a controlled scalar regression task. With phase encoding and strong inter-element mutual coupling (MC), both aforementioned mappings are strongly non-linear and the WPNN performs very well even with a single layer. We further observe that additional layers can partially compensate for weak inter-element MC. In addition, we demonstrate that WPNN depth can improve expressivity even when it is not associated with an increase in trainable weights. Altogether, our results provide a physics-consistent picture of how encoding choice, MC strength, and depth jointly govern the expressive power of PM-based WPNNs, informing design choices for future experimental implementations of WPNNs.

AI.tldr: Wave-based physical neural networks with structural input encoding can achieve strong performance even with single layers when using phase encoding and strong mutual coupling, with depth compensating for weak coupling.
AI.motivation: Conventional wave-based signal processing encodes data into input wavefronts, making non-linear operations challenging. The paper explores an alternative approach using structural input encoding where data is encoded into tunable components' scattering properties, enabling potentially non-linear mappings.
AI.method: Systematic examination of wave-based physical neural network (WPNN) expressivity using a physics-consistent multiport-network model of a D-band rich-scattering cavity with 100-element programmable metasurface. Controlled scalar regression task with separate control of encoding non-linearity, structural non-linearity, and network depth.
AI.result: With phase encoding and strong inter-element mutual coupling, both mappings (input to scattering properties and scattering to output) are strongly non-linear, enabling excellent performance even with single-layer WPNNs. Additional layers can compensate for weak mutual coupling, and depth improves expressivity even without increasing trainable weights.
AI.conclusion: Encoding choice, mutual coupling strength, and depth jointly govern the expressive power of programmable-metasurface-based WPNNs, providing design guidelines for future experimental implementations of wave-based physical neural networks.

title: Safety-Guided Flow (SGF): A Unified Framework for Negative Guidance in Safe Generation
comment: ICLR2026 Oral, Code is available at https://github.com/MingyuKim87/SGF
summary: Safety mechanisms for diffusion and flow models have recently been developed along two distinct paths. In robot planning, control barrier functions are employed to guide generative trajectories away from obstacles at every denoising step by explicitly imposing geometric constraints. In parallel, recent data-driven, negative guidance approaches have been shown to suppress harmful content and promote diversity in generated samples. However, they rely on heuristics without clearly stating when safety guidance is actually necessary. In this paper, we first introduce a unified probabilistic framework using a Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) potential for image generation tasks that recasts both Shielded Diffusion and Safe Denoiser as instances of our energy-based negative guidance against unsafe data samples. Furthermore, we leverage control-barrier functions analysis to justify the existence of a critical time window in which negative guidance must be strong; outside of this window, the guidance should decay to zero to ensure safe and high-quality generation. We evaluate our unified framework on several realistic safe generation scenarios, confirming that negative guidance should be applied in the early stages of the denoising process for successful safe generation.

AI.tldr: Unified probabilistic framework using MMD potential for safe diffusion models that combines control barrier functions and negative guidance, identifying critical early-stage window for effective safety guidance.
AI.motivation: Existing safety approaches for diffusion models are fragmented - robot planning uses control barrier functions with geometric constraints, while data-driven negative guidance uses heuristics without clear timing. Need unified framework with principled timing for safety guidance.
AI.method: Introduces unified probabilistic framework using Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) potential that recasts Shielded Diffusion and Safe Denoiser as energy-based negative guidance. Uses control-barrier function analysis to identify critical time window for safety guidance.
AI.result: Framework shows negative guidance must be strong during early denoising stages (critical time window) and decay to zero outside this window for safe, high-quality generation. Validated on realistic safe generation scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Provides unified theoretical foundation for safe diffusion models with principled timing guidance, demonstrating early-stage negative guidance is crucial for successful safe generation.

title: Continual Fine-Tuning with Provably Accurate and Parameter-Free Task Retrieval
summary: Continual fine-tuning aims to adapt a pre-trained backbone to new tasks sequentially while preserving performance on earlier tasks whose data are no longer available. Existing approaches fall into two categories which include input- and parameter-adaptation. Input-adaptation methods rely on retrieving the most relevant prompts at test time, but require continuously learning a retrieval function that is prone to forgetting. Parameter-adaptation methods instead use a fixed input embedding function to enable retrieval-free prediction and avoid forgetting, but sacrifice representation adaptability. To combine their best strengths, we propose a new parameter-adaptation method that enables adaptive use of input embeddings during test time with parameter-free retrieval. We derive task-retrieval error bounds for a clustering-based, parameter-free paradigm, providing theoretical guarantees that link low retrieval error to structural properties of task-specific representation clusters, revealing a fresh insight into how well-organized clustering structure will enable reliable retrieval. Motivated by this insight, our method is designed with two key components: (i) an adaptive module composition strategy that learns informative task-specific updates to preserve and complement prior knowledge, and (ii) a clustering-based retrieval mechanism that captures distinct representation signatures for each task, enabling adaptive representation use at test time. Extensive experiments show that these components work synergistically to improve retrieval and predictive performance under large shifts in task semantics.

AI.tldr: Proposes a new continual fine-tuning method that combines strengths of input- and parameter-adaptation approaches through adaptive module composition and clustering-based retrieval for better task adaptation without forgetting.
AI.motivation: Existing continual fine-tuning methods have limitations: input-adaptation methods suffer from forgetting due to continuously learning retrieval functions, while parameter-adaptation methods sacrifice representation adaptability by using fixed input embeddings. There's a need to combine their strengths for adaptive representation use without forgetting.
AI.method: Two key components: (1) adaptive module composition strategy that learns informative task-specific updates to preserve and complement prior knowledge, and (2) clustering-based retrieval mechanism that captures distinct representation signatures for each task, enabling adaptive representation use at test time with parameter-free retrieval.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show the components work synergistically to improve both retrieval and predictive performance under large shifts in task semantics, with theoretical guarantees linking low retrieval error to structural properties of task-specific representation clusters.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method successfully combines the best of both input- and parameter-adaptation approaches, enabling adaptive representation use during test time without forgetting, with theoretical insights showing how well-organized clustering structure enables reliable retrieval.

title: Bi-HIL: Bilateral Control-Based Multimodal Hierarchical Imitation Learning via Subtask-Level Progress Rate and Keyframe Memory for Long-Horizon Contact-Rich Robotic Manipulation
summary: Long-horizon contact-rich robotic manipulation remains challenging due to partial observability and unstable subtask transitions under contact uncertainty. While hierarchical architectures improve temporal reasoning and bilateral imitation learning enables force-aware control, existing approaches often rely on flat policies that struggle with long-horizon coordination. We propose Bi-HIL, a bilateral control-based multimodal hierarchical imitation learning framework for long-horizon manipulation. Bi-HIL stabilizes hierarchical coordination by integrating keyframe memory with subtask-level progress rate that models phase progression within the active subtask and conditions both high- and low-level policies. We evaluate Bi-HIL on unimanual and bimanual real-robot tasks, demonstrating consistent improvements over flat and ablated variants. The results highlight the importance of explicitly modeling subtask progression together with force-aware control for robust long-horizon manipulation. For additional material, please check: https://mertcookimg.github.io/bi-hil

AI.tldr: Bi-HIL: A bilateral control-based multimodal hierarchical imitation learning framework that improves long-horizon contact-rich manipulation by integrating keyframe memory with subtask-level progress rate modeling.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon contact-rich robotic manipulation is challenging due to partial observability and unstable subtask transitions under contact uncertainty. Existing approaches often use flat policies that struggle with long-horizon coordination, despite hierarchical architectures improving temporal reasoning and bilateral imitation learning enabling force-aware control.
AI.method: Bi-HIL integrates keyframe memory with subtask-level progress rate modeling that tracks phase progression within active subtasks. This progress rate conditions both high-level and low-level policies, stabilizing hierarchical coordination for long-horizon manipulation tasks.
AI.result: The framework was evaluated on both unimanual and bimanual real-robot tasks, demonstrating consistent improvements over flat policy variants and ablated versions of the approach.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling subtask progression together with force-aware control is crucial for robust long-horizon manipulation. The Bi-HIL framework successfully addresses coordination challenges in contact-rich manipulation tasks.

title: Fundamental Limits of CSI Compression in FDD Massive MIMO
summary: Channel state information (CSI) feedback in frequency-division duplex (FDD) massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) systems is fundamentally limited by the high dimensionality of wideband channels. In this paper, we model the stacked wideband CSI vector as a Gaussian-mixture source with a latent geometry state that represents different propagation environments. Each component corresponds to a locally stationary regime characterized by a correlated proper complex Gaussian distribution with its own covariance matrix. This representation captures the multimodal nature of practical CSI datasets while preserving the analytical tractability of Gaussian models. Motivated by this structure, we propose Gaussian-mixture transform coding (GMTC), a practical CSI feedback architecture that combines state inference with state-adaptive TC. The mixture parameters are learned offline from channel samples and stored as a shared statistical dictionary at both the user equipment (UE) and the base station. For each CSI realization, the UE identifies the most likely geometry state, encodes the corresponding label using a lossless source code, and compresses the CSI using the Karhunen-Loeve transform matched to that state. We further characterize the fundamental limits of CSI compression under this model by deriving analytical converse and achievability bounds on the rate-distortion (RD) function. A key structural result is that the optimal bit allocation across all mixture components is governed by a single global reverse-waterfilling level. Simulations on the COST2100 dataset show that GMTC significantly improves the RD tradeoff relative to neural transform coding approaches while requiring substantially smaller model memory and lower inference complexity. These results indicate that near-optimal CSI compression can be achieved through state-adaptive TC without relying on large neural encoders.

AI.tldr: GMTC: Gaussian-mixture transform coding for CSI feedback in FDD massive MIMO systems that models wideband channels as Gaussian-mixture sources with latent geometry states, enabling state-adaptive compression with improved rate-distortion performance.
AI.motivation: CSI feedback in FDD massive MIMO systems faces fundamental limitations due to high dimensionality of wideband channels. Existing approaches struggle with the multimodal nature of practical CSI datasets while maintaining analytical tractability.
AI.method: Model stacked wideband CSI as Gaussian-mixture source with latent geometry states. Propose GMTC architecture combining state inference with state-adaptive transform coding. Learn mixture parameters offline as shared statistical dictionary. UE identifies most likely geometry state, encodes label losslessly, and compresses CSI using Karhunen-Loeve transform matched to that state.
AI.result: Derived analytical converse and achievability bounds on rate-distortion function. Key structural result: optimal bit allocation across mixture components governed by single global reverse-waterfilling level. Simulations on COST2100 dataset show GMTC significantly improves RD tradeoff relative to neural transform coding while requiring substantially smaller model memory and lower inference complexity.
AI.conclusion: Near-optimal CSI compression can be achieved through state-adaptive transform coding without relying on large neural encoders. GMTC provides practical architecture that captures multimodal nature of CSI datasets while preserving analytical tractability of Gaussian models.

title: Think First, Diffuse Fast: Improving Diffusion Language Model Reasoning via Autoregressive Plan Conditioning
summary: Diffusion large language models (dLLMs) generate text via iterative denoising but consistently underperform on multi-step reasoning. We hypothesize this gap stems from a coordination problem: AR models build coherence token-by-token, while diffusion models must coordinate all positions simultaneously. We propose plan conditioning, a training-free method that prepends a short (~100-token) natural-language plan from an AR model to the diffusion model's prompt. The plan serves as a frozen scaffold -- globally visible context that every token position can attend to from the first denoising step. On GSM8K, plan conditioning improves LLaDA-8B-Instruct from 75.6% to 87.2% (+11.6 percentage points), matching a same-size AR model (LLaMA 3.1 8B, 87.7%) despite a 6.4pp weaker baseline. On HumanEval, the gain is +12.8pp (37.2% to 50.0%), showing plans generalize to code. The same plans improve LLaMA by only +5.7pp on GSM8K and +1.3pp on HumanEval -- diffusion models benefit 2-10x more, supporting the coordination-problem hypothesis. Across 5 random seeds, plan-conditioned GSM8K accuracy has zero standard deviation, making diffusion inference highly stable. Ablations reveal the model follows plan strategy (wrong-strategy plans cause -16.3pp) but is robust to plan values (perturbed numbers: -1.1pp), and that planner quality has a sharp threshold: smaller Llama-class plans hurt (-1.6 to -6.8pp) while frontier plans provide the full lift. Attention analysis confirms the mechanism: plan tokens receive 1.8x excess attention during early denoising, declining to uniform as completion tokens solidify. Plan conditioning costs ~$0.002 per problem and adds ~2s of latency.

AI.tldr: Plan conditioning improves diffusion LLMs' multi-step reasoning by prepending AR-generated natural language plans as global context, achieving AR-level performance with 2-10x greater benefit than AR models.
AI.motivation: Diffusion LLMs underperform on multi-step reasoning compared to autoregressive models, likely due to coordination problems where diffusion models must coordinate all token positions simultaneously while AR models build coherence token-by-token.
AI.method: Training-free plan conditioning: prepend short (~100-token) natural-language plans from AR models to diffusion model prompts. These frozen plans serve as globally visible context that all token positions can attend to from the first denoising step.
AI.result: On GSM8K: LLaDA-8B-Instruct improved from 75.6% to 87.2% (+11.6pp), matching same-size AR model (LLaMA 3.1 8B, 87.7%). On HumanEval: +12.8pp (37.2% to 50.0%). Diffusion models benefit 2-10x more than AR models from same plans. Zero standard deviation across seeds, highly stable inference.
AI.conclusion: Plan conditioning effectively addresses diffusion LLMs' coordination problem in multi-step reasoning, enabling AR-level performance with minimal cost (~$0.002 per problem, ~2s latency). The method is robust to plan value perturbations but sensitive to strategy quality, supporting the coordination hypothesis.

title: MGMAR: Metal-Guided Metal Artifact Reduction for X-ray Computed Tomography
summary: An X-ray computed tomography (CT), metal artifact reduction (MAR) remains a major challenge because metallic implants violate standard CT forward-model assumptions, producing severe streaking and shadowing artifacts that degrade diagnostic quality. We propose MGMAR, a metal-guided MAR method that explicitly leverages metal-related information throughout the reconstruction pipeline. MGMAR first generates a high-quality prior image by training a conditioned implicit neural representation (INR) using metal-unaffected projections, and then incorporates this prior into a normalized MAR (NMAR) framework for projection completion. To improve robustness under severe metal corruption, we pretrain the encoder-conditioned INR on paired metal-corrupted and artifact-free CT images, thereby embedding data-driven prior knowledge into the INR parameter space. This prior-embedded initialization reduces sensitivity to random initialization and accelerates convergence during measurement-specific refinement. The encoder takes a metal-corrupted reconstruction together with a recursively constructed metal artifact image, enabling the latent field to capture metal-dependent global artifact patterns. After projection completion using the INR prior, we further suppress residual artifacts using a metal-conditioned correction network, where the metal mask modulates intermediate features via adaptive instance normalization to target metal-dependent secondary artifacts while preserving anatomical structures. Experiments on the public AAPM-MAR benchmark demonstrate that MGMAR achieves state-of-the-art performance, attaining an average final score of 0.89 on 29 clinical test cases.

AI.tldr: MGMAR is a metal artifact reduction method for CT that uses metal-guided implicit neural representations and encoder-conditioned prior learning to address severe streaking artifacts from metallic implants.
AI.motivation: Metal artifacts in CT imaging violate standard forward-model assumptions, causing severe streaking and shadowing that degrade diagnostic quality. Existing methods struggle with severe metal corruption, requiring better ways to leverage metal-related information throughout reconstruction.
AI.method: 1) Generate prior image using conditioned implicit neural representation (INR) trained on metal-unaffected projections. 2) Incorporate prior into normalized MAR framework for projection completion. 3) Pretrain encoder-conditioned INR on paired metal-corrupted/artifact-free CT images to embed data-driven prior knowledge. 4) Use encoder with metal-corrupted reconstruction and recursively constructed metal artifact image to capture global artifact patterns. 5) Apply metal-conditioned correction network with metal mask modulating features via adaptive instance normalization to suppress residual artifacts.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on AAPM-MAR benchmark with average final score of 0.89 on 29 clinical test cases.
AI.conclusion: MGMAR effectively reduces metal artifacts by explicitly leveraging metal-related information throughout the reconstruction pipeline, combining conditioned implicit neural representations with data-driven prior learning and metal-guided correction networks.

title: Online Model Predictive Control for Trajectory and Beamforming Optimization in UAV-Enabled URLLC
comment: 14 pages, and 12 figures. Submitted to IEEE
summary: This paper investigates joint trajectory and active beamforming design for unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV)-enabled ultra-reliable low-latency communication (URLLC) systems under finite blocklength (FBL) transmission. Unlike conventional Shannon-capacity formulations, the FBL regime introduces a signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR)-dependent dispersion penalty that increases the sensitivity of reliability to mobility-induced channel variations. To address this challenge, we develop a propulsion-aware model predictive control (MPC) framework that performs receding-horizon joint trajectory and multi-user beamforming optimization while enforcing FBL-based rate constraints. The resulting long-horizon nonconvex problem is decomposed into beamforming and trajectory subproblems using alternating optimization. Concave surrogate is constructed for the Shannon-capacity term, while convex approximations are derived for the dispersion term and the nonlinear propulsion power model, yielding tractable convex subproblems solved iteratively. Compared with an offline MPC baseline, where the predictive problem is solved once over the entire mission horizon without feedback updates, and a conventional offline trajectory-beamforming optimization, the proposed closed-loop framework achieves disturbance-resilient mission completion under UAV position disturbances. Simulation results show that, compared with maximum ratio transmission (MRT) and equal-power allocation, the proposed interference-aware design significantly improves URLLC reliability under stringent minimum rate constraints. The results also quantify the impact of antenna scaling, transmit power, and transmission time on FBL performance, providing insights for reliability-centric UAV-enabled wireless networks in 5G and beyond.

AI.tldr: Joint trajectory and beamforming design for UAV-enabled URLLC systems under finite blocklength transmission using MPC framework with disturbance resilience.
AI.motivation: FBL transmission introduces SINR-dependent dispersion penalty that increases sensitivity to mobility-induced channel variations in UAV-enabled URLLC systems, requiring new approaches to maintain reliability under position disturbances.
AI.method: Developed propulsion-aware MPC framework with receding-horizon joint trajectory and multi-user beamforming optimization. Used alternating optimization to decompose into beamforming and trajectory subproblems, with concave surrogate for Shannon-capacity term and convex approximations for dispersion term and propulsion power model.
