AI.method: Three-stage hierarchical approach: 1) Vision-Language Model decomposes instructions into textual subgoals, 2) Fine-tuned generative video model imagines future video trajectory towards subgoal, 3) Inverse dynamics model extracts trajectory from imagined video for low-level controller tracking. Includes scalable data pipeline using auto-labeled in-the-wild navigation videos.
AI.result: Demonstrates strong zero-shot transfer to robot navigation without requiring robot demonstrations, enabling robots to learn navigation directly from unlabeled, open-world data.
AI.conclusion: ImagiNav provides a novel modular paradigm that decouples visual planning from robot actuation, paving the way for generalist robots that can navigate using natural language by learning from diverse real-world video data rather than limited simulation or robot-specific training.

title: State Algebra for Probabilistic Logic
summary:
This paper presents a Probabilistic State Algebra as an extension of deterministic propositional logic, providing a computational framework for constructing Markov Random Fields (MRFs) through pure linear algebra. By mapping logical states to real-valued coordinates interpreted as energy potentials, we define an energy-based model where global probability distributions emerge from coordinate-wise Hadamard products. This approach bypasses the traditional reliance on graph-traversal algorithms and compiled circuits, utilising $t$-objects and wildcards to embed logical reduction natively within matrix operations.
  We demonstrate that this algebra constructs formal Gibbs distributions, offering a rigorous mathematical link between symbolic constraints and statistical inference. A central application of this framework is the development of Probabilistic Rule Models (PRMs), which are uniquely capable of incorporating both probabilistic associations and deterministic logical constraints simultaneously. These models are designed to be inherently interpretable, supporting a human-in-the-loop approach to decisioning in high-stakes environments such as healthcare and finance. By representing decision logic as a modular summation of rules within a vector space, the framework ensures that complex probabilistic systems remain auditable and maintainable without compromising the rigour of the underlying configuration space.

AI.tldr: A Probabilistic State Algebra extends propositional logic to construct Markov Random Fields via linear algebra, creating interpretable probabilistic models with logical constraints.
AI.motivation: To bridge symbolic logic and statistical inference, enabling interpretable probabilistic models that combine logical constraints with probabilistic associations for high-stakes decision-making in fields like healthcare and finance.
AI.method: Extends deterministic propositional logic to probabilistic state algebra, mapping logical states to energy potentials, using linear algebra operations (Hadamard products) to construct Markov Random Fields without graph-traversal algorithms, employing t-objects and wildcards for logical reduction within matrix operations.
AI.result: Develops Probabilistic Rule Models (PRMs) that incorporate both probabilistic associations and deterministic logical constraints simultaneously, creating formal Gibbs distributions with rigorous mathematical links between symbolic constraints and statistical inference.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides inherently interpretable models supporting human-in-the-loop decisioning, ensuring complex probabilistic systems remain auditable and maintainable while preserving the rigor of the underlying configuration space through modular rule representation in vector space.

title: Fast Volume Alignment by Frequency-Marched Newton
summary: We develop a fast and accurate method for 3D alignment, recovering the rotation and translation that best align a reference volume with a noisy observation. Classical matched filtering evaluates cross-correlation over a large discretized transformation space; we show that high-precision alignment can be achieved far more efficiently by treating pose estimation as a continuous optimization problem. Our starting point is a band-limited Wigner-$D$ expansion of the rotational correlation, which enables rapid evaluation and efficient closed-form gradients and Hessians. Combined with analytical control of the complexity of trigonometric-polynomial landscapes, this makes second-order optimization practical in a setting where it is often avoided due to nonconvexity and noise sensitivity. We show that Newton-type refinement is stable and effective when initialized at low angular bandwidth: a coarse low-resolution $\mathrm{SO}(3)$ search provides robust candidates, which are then refined by iterative frequency marching and Newton steps, with translations updated via FFT in an alternating scheme. We provide a deterministic convergence guarantee showing that, under verifiable spectral-decay and gap conditions, the frequency-marching scheme returns a near-optimal solution whose suboptimality is controlled by the Newton tolerance. On synthetic rotation-estimation benchmarks, the method attains sub-degree accuracy while substantially reducing runtime relative to exhaustive $\mathrm{SO}(3)$ search. Integrated into the subtomogram-averaging pipeline of RELION5, it matches the baseline reconstruction quality, reaching local resolution at the Nyquist limit, while reducing pose-refinement time by more than an order of magnitude.

AI.tldr: A fast 3D alignment method using continuous optimization with band-limited Wigner-D expansions, frequency marching, and Newton refinement that achieves sub-degree accuracy while being 10x faster than exhaustive SO(3) search.
AI.motivation: Classical matched filtering for 3D alignment is computationally expensive due to exhaustive search over discretized transformation space. There's a need for more efficient methods that maintain high precision while reducing computational cost.
AI.method: Uses band-limited Wigner-D expansion of rotational correlation for rapid evaluation with closed-form gradients/Hessians. Combines coarse low-resolution SO(3) search with iterative frequency marching and Newton refinement, alternating with FFT-based translation updates.
AI.result: Achieves sub-degree accuracy on synthetic benchmarks with substantial runtime reduction vs exhaustive search. Integrated into RELION5, matches baseline reconstruction quality (Nyquist limit resolution) while reducing pose-refinement time by >10x.
AI.conclusion: Continuous optimization with spectral methods enables fast, accurate 3D alignment with deterministic convergence guarantees, making second-order optimization practical in noisy, nonconvex settings where it was previously avoided.

title: Geometry-Aware Semantic Reasoning for Training Free Video Anomaly Detection
summary: Training-free video anomaly detection (VAD) has recently emerged as a scalable alternative to supervised approaches, yet existing methods largely rely on static prompting and geometry-agnostic feature fusion. As a result, anomaly inference is often reduced to shallow similarity matching over Euclidean embeddings, leading to unstable predictions and limited interpretability, especially in complex or hierarchically structured scenes. We introduce MM-VAD, a geometry-aware semantic reasoning framework for training free VAD that reframes anomaly detection as adaptive test-time inference rather than fixed feature comparison. Our approach projects caption-derived scene representations into hyperbolic space to better preserve hierarchical structure and performs anomaly assessment through an adaptive question answering process over a frozen large language model. A lightweight, learnable prompt is optimised at test time using an unsupervised confidence-sparsity objective, enabling context-specific calibration without updating any backbone parameters. To further ground semantic predictions in visual evidence, we incorporate a covariance-aware Mahalanobis refinement that stabilises cross-modal alignment. Across four benchmarks, MM-VAD consistently improves over prior training-free methods, achieving 90.03% AUC on XD-Violence and 83.24%, 96.95%, and 98.81% on UCF-Crime, ShanghaiTech, and UCSD Ped2, respectively. Our results demonstrate that geometry-aware representation and adaptive semantic calibration provide a principled and effective alternative to static Euclidean matching in training-free VAD.

AI.tldr: MM-VAD: A training-free video anomaly detection framework that uses hyperbolic space for hierarchical scene representation and adaptive question answering with LLMs, outperforming prior methods on multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing training-free VAD methods rely on static prompting and geometry-agnostic feature fusion, leading to unstable predictions and limited interpretability in complex scenes. There's a need for more principled approaches that better handle hierarchical scene structure.
AI.method: Projects caption-derived scene representations into hyperbolic space to preserve hierarchical structure, performs anomaly assessment through adaptive question answering over frozen LLMs, optimizes lightweight prompts at test time using unsupervised confidence-sparsity objective, and incorporates covariance-aware Mahalanobis refinement for cross-modal alignment.
AI.result: Achieves 90.03% AUC on XD-Violence, 83.24% on UCF-Crime, 96.95% on ShanghaiTech, and 98.81% on UCSD Ped2, consistently outperforming prior training-free methods.
AI.conclusion: Geometry-aware representation and adaptive semantic calibration provide a principled and effective alternative to static Euclidean matching in training-free VAD, enabling better handling of complex hierarchical scenes.

title: Enhanced Atrial Fibrillation Prediction in ESUS Patients with Hypergraph-based Pre-training
summary: Atrial fibrillation (AF) is a major complication following embolic stroke of undetermined source (ESUS), elevating the risk of recurrent stroke and mortality. Early identification is clinically important, yet existing tools face limitations in accuracy, scalability, and cost. Machine learning (ML) offers promise but is hindered by small ESUS cohorts and high-dimensional medical features. To address these challenges, we introduce supervised and unsupervised hypergraph-based pre-training strategies to improve AF prediction in ESUS patients. We first pre-train hypergraph-based patient embedding models on a large stroke cohort (7,780 patients) to capture salient features and higher-order interactions. The resulting embeddings are transferred to a smaller ESUS cohort (510 patients), reducing feature dimensionality while preserving clinically meaningful information, enabling effective prediction with lightweight models. Experiments show that both pre-training approaches outperform traditional models trained on raw data, improving accuracy and robustness. This framework offers a scalable and efficient solution for AF risk prediction after stroke.

AI.tldr: Hypergraph-based pre-training on large stroke cohort improves atrial fibrillation prediction in embolic stroke patients with limited data
AI.motivation: Atrial fibrillation (AF) is a major complication after embolic stroke of undetermined source (ESUS), increasing recurrent stroke and mortality risk. Existing prediction tools have accuracy, scalability, and cost limitations, while machine learning approaches are hindered by small ESUS cohorts and high-dimensional medical features.
AI.method: Introduce supervised and unsupervised hypergraph-based pre-training strategies. First pre-train hypergraph-based patient embedding models on large stroke cohort (7,780 patients) to capture salient features and higher-order interactions. Transfer embeddings to smaller ESUS cohort (510 patients), reducing feature dimensionality while preserving clinically meaningful information for lightweight prediction models.
AI.result: Both pre-training approaches outperform traditional models trained on raw data, improving accuracy and robustness for AF prediction in ESUS patients.
AI.conclusion: The hypergraph-based pre-training framework offers a scalable and efficient solution for AF risk prediction after stroke, addressing data limitations and high-dimensional feature challenges in clinical settings.

title: Fine-tuning is Not Enough: A Parallel Framework for Collaborative Imitation and Reinforcement Learning in End-to-end Autonomous Driving
summary: End-to-end autonomous driving is typically built upon imitation learning (IL), yet its performance is constrained by the quality of human demonstrations. To overcome this limitation, recent methods incorporate reinforcement learning (RL) through sequential fine-tuning. However, such a paradigm remains suboptimal: sequential RL fine-tuning can introduce policy drift and often leads to a performance ceiling due to its dependence on the pretrained IL policy. To address these issues, we propose PaIR-Drive, a general Parallel framework for collaborative Imitation and Reinforcement learning in end-to-end autonomous driving. During training, PaIR-Drive separates IL and RL into two parallel branches with conflict-free training objectives, enabling fully collaborative optimization. This design eliminates the need to retrain RL when applying a new IL policy. During inference, RL leverages the IL policy to further optimize the final plan, allowing performance beyond prior knowledge of IL. Furthermore, we introduce a tree-structured trajectory neural sampler to group relative policy optimization (GRPO) in the RL branch, which enhances exploration capability. Extensive analysis on NAVSIMv1 and v2 benchmark demonstrates that PaIR-Drive achieves Competitive performance of 91.2 PDMS and 87.9 EPDMS, building upon Transfuser and DiffusionDrive IL baselines. PaIR-Drive consistently outperforms existing RL fine-tuning methods, and could even correct human experts' suboptimal behaviors. Qualitative results further confirm that PaIR-Drive can effectively explore and generate high-quality trajectories.

AI.tldr: PaIR-Drive introduces a parallel framework for collaborative imitation and reinforcement learning in autonomous driving, eliminating sequential fine-tuning issues and enabling better performance than human demonstrations.
AI.motivation: Current end-to-end autonomous driving methods rely on imitation learning (IL) limited by human demonstration quality. Sequential RL fine-tuning causes policy drift and performance ceilings due to dependence on pretrained IL policies.
AI.method: PaIR-Drive separates IL and RL into parallel branches with conflict-free objectives, enabling collaborative optimization. It uses a tree-structured trajectory neural sampler for grouped relative policy optimization (GRPO) in RL branch to enhance exploration.
AI.result: Achieves competitive performance of 91.2 PDMS and 87.9 EPDMS on NAVSIM benchmarks, outperforms existing RL fine-tuning methods, and can correct human experts' suboptimal behaviors.
AI.conclusion: PaIR-Drive provides an effective parallel framework that overcomes limitations of sequential IL+RL approaches, enabling collaborative optimization and performance beyond human demonstration quality in autonomous driving.

title: EnterpriseOps-Gym: Environments and Evaluations for Stateful Agentic Planning and Tool Use in Enterprise Settings
summary: Large language models are shifting from passive information providers to active agents intended for complex workflows. However, their deployment as reliable AI workers in enterprise is stalled by benchmarks that fail to capture the intricacies of professional environments, specifically, the need for long-horizon planning amidst persistent state changes and strict access protocols. In this work, we introduce EnterpriseOps-Gym, a benchmark designed to evaluate agentic planning in realistic enterprise settings. Specifically, EnterpriseOps-Gym features a containerized sandbox with 164 database tables and 512 functional tools to mimic real-world search friction. Within this environment, agents are evaluated on 1,150 expert-curated tasks across eight mission-critical verticals (including Customer Service, HR, and IT). Our evaluation of 14 frontier models reveals critical limitations in state-of-the-art models: the top-performing Claude Opus 4.5 achieves only 37.4% success. Further analysis shows that providing oracle human plans improves performance by 14-35 percentage points, pinpointing strategic reasoning as the primary bottleneck. Additionally, agents frequently fail to refuse infeasible tasks (best model achieves 53.9%), leading to unintended and potentially harmful side effects. Our findings underscore that current agents are not yet ready for autonomous enterprise deployment. More broadly, EnterpriseOps-Gym provides a concrete testbed to advance the robustness of agentic planning in professional workflows.

AI.tldr: EnterpriseOps-Gym is a benchmark for evaluating AI agents in realistic enterprise environments with complex workflows, showing current models struggle with strategic planning and task refusal.
AI.motivation: Current benchmarks fail to capture the complexities of professional enterprise environments where AI agents need long-horizon planning, handle persistent state changes, and navigate strict access protocols. There's a gap in evaluating agentic planning for reliable enterprise deployment.
AI.method: Created EnterpriseOps-Gym benchmark featuring: 1) Containerized sandbox environment, 2) 164 database tables and 512 functional tools to simulate real-world search friction, 3) 1,150 expert-curated tasks across 8 mission-critical verticals (Customer Service, HR, IT, etc.), 4) Evaluation of 14 frontier language models.
AI.result: Top-performing Claude Opus 4.5 achieved only 37.4% success rate. Providing oracle human plans improved performance by 14-35 percentage points, indicating strategic reasoning is the primary bottleneck. Agents also struggled to refuse infeasible tasks (best model: 53.9%), potentially causing harmful side effects.
AI.conclusion: Current AI agents are not yet ready for autonomous enterprise deployment. EnterpriseOps-Gym provides a concrete testbed to advance agentic planning robustness in professional workflows, highlighting critical limitations in strategic reasoning and task refusal capabilities.

title: On the CRLB for Blind Receiver I/Q Imbalance Estimation in OFDM Systems: Efficient Computation and Closed-Form Bounds
comment: 12 pages, 4 figures, extended version of our work presented at the 2025 Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
summary: Modern mobile communication receivers are often implemented with a direct-conversion architecture, which features a number of advantages over competing designs. A notable limitation of direct-conversion architectures, however, is their sensitivity to amplitude and phase mismatches between the in-phase and quadrature signal paths. Such in-phase and quadrature-phase (I/Q) imbalances introduce undesired image components in the baseband signal, degrading link performance -- most notably by increasing the bit-error ratio. Considerable research effort has therefore been devoted to digital techniques for estimating and mitigating these impairments. Existing approaches generally fall into two categories: data-aided methods that exploit known pilots, preambles, or training sequences, and blind techniques that operate without such prior information. For data-aided estimation, Cramér-Rao lower bounds (CRLBs) have been established in the literature. In contrast, the derivation of a CRLB for the blind I/Q-imbalance estimation case is considerably more challenging, since the received data is random and typically non-Gaussian in the frequency domain. This work extends our earlier conference contribution, which introduced a CRLB derivation for the blind estimation of frequency-independent (FID) receiver I/Q imbalance using central limit theorem (CLT) arguments. The extensions include a computationally efficient method for calculating the bound, reducing complexity from cubic in the number of samples to linear in the fast-Fourier transform (FFT) size, along with a simplified closed-form approximation. This approximation provides new insights into the allocation dependent performances of existing estimation methods, motivating a pre-estimation filtering modification that drastically improves their estimation performance in certain scenarios.

AI.tldr: Derives Cramér-Rao lower bound for blind estimation of I/Q imbalance in direct-conversion receivers, with efficient computation methods and practical insights for improved estimation performance.
AI.motivation: Direct-conversion receivers suffer from I/Q imbalances that degrade link performance, but existing CRLBs only exist for data-aided methods, not for blind estimation which is more challenging due to random, non-Gaussian frequency-domain data.
AI.method: Extends previous conference work on CRLB derivation for blind frequency-independent I/Q imbalance estimation using central limit theorem arguments. Introduces computationally efficient method reducing complexity from O(N³) to O(FFT size), plus a simplified closed-form approximation.
AI.result: Provides new CRLB for blind I/Q imbalance estimation, enabling performance analysis of existing methods. The approximation reveals allocation-dependent performance characteristics, motivating a pre-estimation filtering modification that significantly improves estimation in certain scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The derived CRLB and efficient computation methods provide theoretical foundation for analyzing blind I/Q imbalance estimation performance, with practical implications for improving existing estimation techniques through pre-estimation filtering.

title: InfiniteDance: Scalable 3D Dance Generation Towards in-the-wild Generalization
summary: Although existing 3D dance generation methods perform well in controlled scenarios, they often struggle to generalize in the wild. When conditioned on unseen music, existing methods often produce unstructured or physically implausible dance, largely due to limited music-to-dance data and restricted model capacity. This work aims to push the frontier of generalizable 3D dance generation by scaling up both data and model design. (1) On the data side, we develop a fully automated pipeline that reconstructs high-fidelity 3D dance motions from monocular videos. To eliminate the physical artifacts prevalent in existing reconstruction methods, we introduce a Foot Restoration Diffusion Model (FRDM) guided by foot-contact and geometric constraints that enforce physical plausibility while preserving kinematic smoothness and expressiveness, resulting in a diverse, high-quality multimodal 3D dance dataset totaling 100.69 hours. (2) On model design, we propose Choreographic LLaMA (ChoreoLLaMA), a scalable LLaMA-based architecture. To enhance robustness under unfamiliar music conditions, we integrate a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) module that injects reference dance as a prompt. Additionally, we design a slow/fast-cadence Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) module that enables ChoreoLLaMA to smoothly adapt motion rhythms across varying music tempos. Extensive experiments across diverse dance genres show that our approach surpasses existing methods in both qualitative and quantitative evaluations, marking a step toward scalable, real-world 3D dance generation. Code, models, and data will be released.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a scalable approach for 3D dance generation that addresses generalization issues by scaling up both data (via automated reconstruction with physical plausibility constraints) and model design (using a LLaMA-based architecture with RAG and MoE modules).
AI.motivation: Existing 3D dance generation methods struggle to generalize to unseen music, producing unstructured or physically implausible dance due to limited music-to-dance data and restricted model capacity.
AI.method: Two main contributions: (1) Data scaling: automated pipeline for high-fidelity 3D dance reconstruction from monocular videos using Foot Restoration Diffusion Model (FRDM) with physical constraints; (2) Model design: Choreographic LLaMA (ChoreoLLaMA) architecture with retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) module for robustness and slow/fast-cadence Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) for tempo adaptation.
AI.result: Created a diverse, high-quality multimodal 3D dance dataset of 100.69 hours. Extensive experiments across diverse dance genres show the approach surpasses existing methods in both qualitative and quantitative evaluations.
AI.conclusion: The work marks a step toward scalable, real-world 3D dance generation by addressing generalization challenges through data and model scaling, with improved physical plausibility and robustness to unfamiliar music conditions.

title: FusionCast: Enhancing Precipitation Nowcasting with Asymmetric Cross-Modal Fusion and Future Radar Priors
summary: Deep learning has significantly improved the accuracy of precipitation nowcasting. However, most existing multimodal models typically use simple channel concatenation or interpolation methods for data fusion, which often overlook the feature differences between different modalities. This paper therefore proposes a novel precipitation nowcasting optimisation framework called FusionCast. This framework incorporates three types of data: historical precipitable water vapour (PWV) data derived from global navigation satellite system (GNSS) inversions, historical radar based quantitative precipitation estimation (QPE), and forecasted radar QPE serving as a future prior. The FusionCast model comprises two core modules: the future prior radar QPE processing Module, which forecasts future radar data; and the Radar PWV Fusion (RPF) module, which uses a gate mechanism to efficiently combine features from various sources. Experimental results show that FusionCast significantly improves nowcasting performance.

AI.tldr: FusionCast: A novel precipitation nowcasting framework using GNSS-derived PWV data, historical radar QPE, and forecasted radar QPE with gated fusion mechanism for improved accuracy.
AI.motivation: Most existing multimodal precipitation nowcasting models use simple data fusion methods (channel concatenation or interpolation) that overlook feature differences between different modalities, limiting their performance.
AI.method: Proposes FusionCast framework with three data types: historical PWV from GNSS inversions, historical radar QPE, and forecasted radar QPE as future prior. Includes two core modules: future prior radar QPE processing module for forecasting future radar data, and Radar PWV Fusion (RPF) module using gate mechanism to efficiently combine features from various sources.
AI.result: Experimental results show that FusionCast significantly improves nowcasting performance compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed FusionCast framework with its gated fusion mechanism effectively addresses the limitations of simple multimodal fusion methods and demonstrates superior precipitation nowcasting accuracy.

title: LDHP: Library-Driven Hierarchical Planning for Non-prehensile Dexterous Manipulation
summary: Non-prehensile manipulation is essential for handling thin, large, or otherwise ungraspable objects in unstructured settings. Prior planning and search-based methods often rely on ad-hoc manual designs or generate physically unrealizable motions by ignoring critical gripper properties, while training-based approaches are data-intensive and struggle to generalize to novel, out-of-distribution tasks. We propose a library-driven hierarchical planner (LDHP) that makes executability a first-class design goal: a top-tier contact-state planner proposes object-pose paths using MoveObject primitives, and a bottom-tier grasp planner synthesizes feasible grasp sequences with AdjustGrasp primitives; feasibility is certified by collision checks and quasi-static mechanics, and contact-sensitive segments are recovered via a bounded dichotomy refinement. This gripper-aware decomposition decouples object motion from grasp realizability, yields a task-agnostic pipeline that transfers across manipulation tasks and geometric variations without re-design, and exposes clean hooks for optional learned priors. Real-robot studies on zero-mobility lifting and slot insertion demonstrate consistent execution and robustness to shape and environment changes.

AI.tldr: LDHP is a hierarchical planner for non-prehensile manipulation that ensures executability by separating object motion planning from grasp feasibility checking, enabling robust transfer across tasks without redesign.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for non-prehensile manipulation have limitations: planning/search methods use ad-hoc designs or generate unrealizable motions, while training-based approaches are data-intensive and don't generalize well to novel tasks.
AI.method: Library-driven hierarchical planner with two tiers: top-tier contact-state planner proposes object-pose paths using MoveObject primitives, bottom-tier grasp planner synthesizes feasible grasp sequences with AdjustGrasp primitives. Uses collision checks, quasi-static mechanics for feasibility certification, and bounded dichotomy refinement for contact-sensitive segments.
AI.result: Real-robot studies on zero-mobility lifting and slot insertion demonstrate consistent execution and robustness to shape and environment changes. The approach transfers across manipulation tasks and geometric variations without redesign.
AI.conclusion: LDHP provides a gripper-aware decomposition that decouples object motion from grasp realizability, creating a task-agnostic pipeline that exposes clean hooks for optional learned priors while ensuring physical feasibility.

title: Orla: A Library for Serving LLM-Based Multi-Agent Systems
summary: We introduce Orla, a library for constructing and running LLM-based agentic systems. Modern agentic applications consist of workflows that combine multiple LLM inference steps, tool calls, and heterogeneous infrastructure. Today, developers typically build these systems by manually composing orchestration code with LLM serving engines and tool execution logic. Orla provides a general abstraction that separates request execution from workflow-level policy. It acts as a serving layer above existing LLM inference engines: developers define workflows composed of stages, while Orla manages how those stages are mapped, executed, and coordinated across models and backends. It provides agent-level control through three mechanisms: a stage mapper, which assigns each stage to an appropriate model and backend; a workflow orchestrator, which schedules stages and manages their resources and context; and a memory manager, which manages inference state such as the KV cache across workflow boundaries. We demonstrate Orla with a customer support workflow that exercises many of its capabilities. We evaluate Orla on two datasets, showing that stage mapping improves latency and cost compared to a single-model vLLM baseline, while workflow-level cache management reduces time-to-first-token.

AI.tldr: Orla is a library for building and running LLM-based agentic systems that separates workflow execution from policy, providing stage mapping, workflow orchestration, and memory management to improve performance and reduce costs.
AI.motivation: Modern agentic applications involve complex workflows combining multiple LLM inference steps, tool calls, and heterogeneous infrastructure. Currently, developers manually compose orchestration code with LLM serving engines and tool execution logic, which is inefficient and lacks proper abstractions.
AI.method: Orla provides a serving layer abstraction above existing LLM inference engines. Developers define workflows as stages, while Orla manages execution through three mechanisms: 1) stage mapper (assigns stages to appropriate models/backends), 2) workflow orchestrator (schedules stages and manages resources/context), and 3) memory manager (handles inference state like KV cache across workflow boundaries).
AI.result: Evaluation on two datasets shows that Orla's stage mapping improves latency and cost compared to single-model vLLM baseline, while workflow-level cache management reduces time-to-first-token. Demonstrated with a customer support workflow exercising many capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Orla successfully provides a general abstraction for LLM-based agentic systems that separates execution from workflow policy, enabling better performance, cost efficiency, and developer productivity through its three-component architecture.

title: Near-field Boundary Distance in mmWave and THz Communications with Misaligned Antenna Arrays
summary: Wireless communications in the millimeter wave (mmWave) and terahertz (THz) spectrum allow harnessing large frequency bands, thus achieving ultra-high data rates. However, the inherently short wavelengths of mmWave and THz signals lead to an extended radiative near-field region, where certain canonical far-field assumptions fail. Most prior works aimed to characterize this radiative near-field region either do not consider antenna arrays on both communicating nodes or, if they do, assume perfect alignment between the arrays. However, such assumptions break down in many realistic deployments, where both sides must employ large-scale mmWave/THz antenna arrays to maintain the desired communication range, while perfect antenna alignment cannot be guaranteed particularly under nodes mobility. In this work, a generalized mathematical framework is presented to characterize the radiative near-field distance in directional mmWave and THz communication systems under various realistic array rotations and misalignments. With the use of the developed framework, compact closed-form expressions are derived for the near-field boundary distance in a wide range of antenna configurations, including array-to-array and array-to-point setups, considering both linear and planar arrays. Our numerical study reveals that the presence of antenna misalignment may significantly adjust the boundaries of the near-field region in mmWave and THz communication systems.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a mathematical framework to characterize the radiative near-field region in mmWave/THz systems considering realistic antenna misalignments, deriving closed-form expressions for near-field boundaries across various array configurations.
AI.motivation: Prior works on mmWave/THz near-field characterization either ignore antenna arrays on both nodes or assume perfect alignment, which breaks down in realistic deployments where both sides need large arrays for range and perfect alignment cannot be guaranteed under mobility.
AI.method: The paper presents a generalized mathematical framework to characterize radiative near-field distance in directional mmWave/THz systems under various realistic array rotations and misalignments. It derives compact closed-form expressions for near-field boundary distance across array-to-array and array-to-point setups with both linear and planar arrays.
AI.result: Numerical study reveals that antenna misalignment can significantly adjust the boundaries of the near-field region in mmWave and THz communication systems. The framework provides practical tools for system design considering realistic deployment conditions.
AI.conclusion: The developed framework addresses the limitations of prior assumptions about perfect antenna alignment, providing more accurate characterization of near-field boundaries in realistic mmWave/THz deployments where misalignments are common, particularly under mobility conditions.

title: A Computer-aided Framework for Detecting Osteosarcoma in Computed Tomography Scans
comment: 12 pages, Presented at The 2nd workshop about High-Performance e-Science
summary: Osteosarcoma is the most common primary bone cancer, mainly affecting the youngest and oldest populations. Its detection at early stages is crucial to reduce the probability of developing bone metastasis. In this context, accurate and fast diagnosis is essential to help physicians during the prognosis process. The research goal is to automate the diagnosis of osteosarcoma through a pipeline that includes the preprocessing, detection, postprocessing, and visualization of computed tomography (CT) scans. Thus, this paper presents a machine learning and visualization framework for classifying CT scans using different convolutional neural network (CNN) models. Preprocessing includes data augmentation and identification of the region of interest in scans. Post-processing includes data visualization to render a 3D bone model that highlights the affected area. An evaluation on 12 patients revealed the effectiveness of our framework, obtaining an area under the curve (AUC) of 94.8\% and a specificity of 94.6\%.

AI.tldr: A machine learning and visualization framework for automated osteosarcoma diagnosis from CT scans using CNN models with 3D visualization of affected bone areas.
AI.motivation: Osteosarcoma is the most common primary bone cancer affecting young and old populations, and early detection is crucial to reduce metastasis risk. Accurate and fast diagnosis is needed to assist physicians in prognosis.
AI.method: A pipeline framework with preprocessing (data augmentation, ROI identification), detection using various CNN models for CT scan classification, postprocessing, and 3D visualization to highlight affected bone areas.
AI.result: Evaluation on 12 patients showed high effectiveness with 94.8% AUC and 94.6% specificity for osteosarcoma classification.
AI.conclusion: The proposed machine learning and visualization framework effectively automates osteosarcoma diagnosis from CT scans, providing accurate classification and 3D visualization of affected areas to assist physicians.

title: DreamReader: An Interpretability Toolkit for Text-to-Image Models
summary:
Despite the rapid adoption of text-to-image (T2I) diffusion models, causal and representation-level analysis remains fragmented and largely limited to isolated probing techniques. To address this gap, we introduce DreamReader: a unified framework that formalizes diffusion interpretability as composable representation operators spanning activation extraction, causal patching, structured ablations, and activation steering across modules and timesteps. DreamReader provides a model-agnostic abstraction layer enabling systematic analysis and intervention across diffusion architectures. Beyond consolidating existing methods, DreamReader introduces three novel intervention primitives for diffusion models: (1) representation fine-tuning (LoReFT) for subspace-constrained internal adaptation; (2) classifier-guided gradient steering using MLP probes trained on activations; and (3) component-level cross-model mapping for systematic study of transferability of representations across modalities. These mechanisms allows us to do lightweight white-box interventions on T2I models by drawing inspiration from interpretability techniques on LLMs.
  We demonstrate DreamReader through controlled experiments that (i) perform activation stitching between two models, and (ii) apply LoReFT to steer multiple activation units, reliably injecting a target concept into the generated images. Experiments are specified declaratively and executed in controlled batched pipelines to enable reproducible large-scale analysis. Across multiple case studies, we show that techniques adapted from language model interpretability yield promising and controllable interventions in diffusion models. DreamReader is released as an open source toolkit for advancing research on T2I interpretability.

AI.tldr: DreamReader is a unified framework for interpretability analysis of text-to-image diffusion models, offering composable representation operators and novel intervention primitives for systematic causal analysis and lightweight white-box interventions.
AI.motivation: Current interpretability analysis for text-to-image diffusion models is fragmented and limited to isolated probing techniques, lacking a unified framework for systematic causal and representation-level analysis across different architectures.
AI.method: DreamReader provides a model-agnostic abstraction layer with composable representation operators including activation extraction, causal patching, structured ablations, and activation steering. It introduces three novel intervention primitives: representation fine-tuning (LoReFT) for subspace-constrained adaptation, classifier-guided gradient steering using MLP probes, and component-level cross-model mapping for studying representation transferability across modalities.
AI.result: The framework enables controlled experiments including activation stitching between models and LoReFT-based concept injection into generated images. Techniques adapted from language model interpretability yield promising and controllable interventions in diffusion models, with experiments executed in reproducible batched pipelines.
AI.conclusion: DreamReader successfully bridges interpretability techniques from language models to diffusion models, providing a unified open-source toolkit for advancing text-to-image interpretability research through systematic analysis and lightweight white-box interventions.

title: TransDex: Pre-training Visuo-Tactile Policy with Point Cloud Reconstruction for Dexterous Manipulation of Transparent Objects
summary: Dexterous manipulation enables complex tasks but suffers from self-occlusion, severe depth noise, and depth information loss when manipulating transparent objects. To solve this problem, this paper proposes TransDex, a 3D visuo-tactile fusion motor policy based on point cloud reconstruction pre-training. Specifically, we first propose a self-supervised point cloud reconstruction pre-training approach based on Transformer. This method accurately recovers the 3D structure of objects from interactive point clouds of dexterous hands, even when random noise and large-scale masking are added. Building on this, TransDex is constructed in which perceptual encoding adopts a fine-grained hierarchical scheme and multi-round attention mechanisms adaptively fuse features of the robotic arm and dexterous hand to enable differentiated motion prediction. Results from transparent object manipulation experiments conducted on a real robotic system demonstrate that TransDex outperforms existing baseline methods. Further analysis validates the generalization capabilities of TransDex and the effectiveness of its individual components.

AI.tldr: TransDex: A 3D visuo-tactile fusion policy using point cloud reconstruction pre-training for transparent object manipulation with dexterous hands.
AI.motivation: Dexterous manipulation suffers from self-occlusion, severe depth noise, and depth information loss when manipulating transparent objects, which existing methods struggle to handle effectively.
AI.method: Proposes TransDex with: 1) Self-supervised point cloud reconstruction pre-training using Transformer to recover 3D structure from noisy/masked interactive point clouds; 2) Fine-grained hierarchical perceptual encoding; 3) Multi-round attention mechanisms for adaptive fusion of robotic arm and dexterous hand features for differentiated motion prediction.
AI.result: TransDex outperforms existing baseline methods in transparent object manipulation experiments on a real robotic system, demonstrating superior performance and generalization capabilities.
AI.conclusion: TransDex effectively addresses transparent object manipulation challenges through 3D visuo-tactile fusion with point cloud reconstruction pre-training, showing practical value for real-world robotic applications.

title: LLM Routing as Reasoning: A MaxSAT View
summary: Routing a query through an appropriate LLM is challenging, particularly when user preferences are expressed in natural language and model attributes are only partially observable. We propose a constraint-based interpretation of language-conditioned LLM routing, formulating it as a weighted MaxSAT/MaxSMT problem in which natural language feedback induces hard and soft constraints over model attributes. Under this view, routing corresponds to selecting models that approximately maximize satisfaction of feedback-conditioned clauses. Empirical analysis on a 25-model benchmark shows that language feedback produces near-feasible recommendation sets, while no-feedback scenarios reveal systematic priors. Our results suggest that LLM routing can be understood as structured constraint optimization under language-conditioned preferences.

AI.tldr: LLM routing with natural language feedback is formulated as weighted MaxSAT/MaxSMT constraint optimization problem.
AI.motivation: Routing queries to appropriate LLMs is challenging when user preferences are expressed in natural language and model attributes are only partially observable. Existing approaches struggle with language-conditioned preferences.
AI.method: Propose constraint-based interpretation of language-conditioned LLM routing as weighted MaxSAT/MaxSMT problem. Natural language feedback induces hard and soft constraints over model attributes. Routing selects models that approximately maximize satisfaction of feedback-conditioned clauses.
AI.result: Empirical analysis on 25-model benchmark shows language feedback produces near-feasible recommendation sets. No-feedback scenarios reveal systematic priors in model selection.
AI.conclusion: LLM routing can be understood as structured constraint optimization under language-conditioned preferences, providing formal framework for language-based model selection.

title: Vision-Language Based Expert Reporting for Painting Authentication and Defect Detection
comment: Submitted to Journal of Cultural Heritage
summary: Authenticity and condition assessment are central to conservation decision-making, yet interpretation and reporting of thermographic output remain largely bespoke and expert-dependent, complicating comparison across collections and limiting systematic integration into conservation documentation. Pulsed Active Infrared Thermography (AIRT) is sensitive to subsurface features such as material heterogeneity, voids, and past interventions; however, its broader adoption is constrained by artifact misinterpretation, inter-laboratory variability, and the absence of standardized, explainable reporting frameworks. Although multi-modal thermographic processing techniques are established, their integration with structured natural-language interpretation has not been explored in cultural heritage. A fully automated thermography-vision-language model (VLM) framework is presented. It combines multi-modal AIRT analysis with modality-aware textual reporting, without human intervention during inference. Thermal sequences are processed using Principal Component Thermography (PCT), Thermographic Signal Reconstruction (TSR), and Pulsed Phase Thermography (PPT), and the resulting anomaly masks are fused into a consensus segmentation that emphasizes regions supported by multiple thermal indicators while mitigating boundary artifacts. The fused evidence is provided to a VLM, which generates structured reports describing the location of the anomaly, thermal behavior, and plausible physical interpretations while explicitly acknowledging the uncertainty and diagnostic limitations. Evaluation on two marquetries demonstrates consistent anomaly detection and stable structured interpretations, indicating reproducibility and generalizability across samples.

AI.tldr: Automated thermography-vision-language framework for cultural heritage combines multi-modal thermal analysis with structured natural-language reporting to standardize interpretation of subsurface anomalies.
AI.motivation: Current thermographic analysis in cultural heritage conservation is bespoke, expert-dependent, and lacks standardization, making comparisons across collections difficult and limiting systematic integration into conservation documentation.
AI.method: Fully automated framework combining multi-modal AIRT analysis (PCT, TSR, PPT) with modality-aware textual reporting using a Vision-Language Model. Thermal sequences are processed, anomaly masks fused into consensus segmentation, and VLM generates structured reports describing anomalies, thermal behavior, and physical interpretations with uncertainty acknowledgment.
AI.result: Evaluation on two marquetries shows consistent anomaly detection and stable structured interpretations, demonstrating reproducibility and generalizability across samples.
AI.conclusion: The automated thermography-VLM framework addresses standardization challenges in cultural heritage thermography, enabling reproducible, explainable reporting that can facilitate broader adoption and systematic integration into conservation practice.

title: Deep Learning for BioImaging: What Are We Learning?
summary: Representation learning has driven major advances in natural image analysis by enabling models to acquire high-level semantic features. In microscopy imaging, however, it remains unclear what current representation learning methods actually learn. In this work, we conduct a systematic study of representation learning for the two most widely used and broadly available microscopy data types, representing critical scales in biology: cell culture and tissue imaging. To this end, we introduce a set of simple yet revealing baselines on curated benchmarks, including untrained models and simple structural representations of cellular tissue. Our results show that, surprisingly, state-of-the-art methods perform comparably to these baselines. We further show that, in contrast to natural images, existing models fail to consistently acquire high-level, biologically meaningful features. Moreover, we demonstrate that commonly used benchmark metrics are insufficient to assess representation quality and often mask this limitation. In addition, we investigate how detailed comparisons with these benchmarks provide ways to interpret the strengths and weaknesses of models for further improvements. Together, our results suggest that progress in microscopy image representation learning requires not only stronger models, but also more diagnostic benchmarks that measure what is actually learned.

AI.tldr: Current representation learning methods for microscopy images perform comparably to simple baselines and fail to consistently learn biologically meaningful features, with standard benchmarks masking these limitations.
AI.motivation: While representation learning has advanced natural image analysis, it remains unclear what current methods actually learn from microscopy images, which represent critical biological scales from cell culture to tissue imaging.
AI.method: Systematic study of representation learning for microscopy images using curated benchmarks and simple baselines including untrained models and structural representations of cellular tissue.
AI.result: State-of-the-art methods perform comparably to simple baselines, fail to consistently acquire high-level biologically meaningful features, and commonly used benchmark metrics mask these limitations.
AI.conclusion: Progress in microscopy image representation learning requires both stronger models and more diagnostic benchmarks that actually measure what is learned.

title: Path-conditioned Reinforcement Learning-based Local Planning for Long-Range Navigation
summary: Long-range navigation is commonly addressed through hierarchical pipelines in which a global planner generates a path, decomposed into waypoints, and followed sequentially by a local planner. These systems are sensitive to global path quality, as inaccurate remote sensing data can result in locally infeasible waypoints, which degrade local execution. At the same time, the limited global context available to the local planner hinders long-range efficiency. To address this issue, we propose a reinforcement learning-based local navigation policy that leverages path information as contextual guidance. The policy is conditioned on reference path observations and trained with a reward function mainly based on goal-reaching objectives, without any explicit path-following reward. Through this implicit conditioning, the policy learns to opportunistically exploit path information while remaining robust to misleading or degraded guidance. Experimental results show that the proposed approach significantly improves navigation efficiency when high-quality paths are available and maintains baseline-level performance when path observations are severely degraded or even non-existent. These properties make the method particularly well-suited for long-range navigation scenarios in which high-level plans are approximate and local execution must remain adaptive to uncertainty.

AI.tldr: RL-based local navigation policy that uses path information as contextual guidance without explicit path-following rewards, maintaining robustness to poor guidance while improving efficiency with good paths.
AI.motivation: Hierarchical navigation pipelines are sensitive to global path quality - inaccurate remote sensing leads to infeasible waypoints that degrade local execution, while limited global context hinders long-range efficiency.
AI.method: Reinforcement learning-based local navigation policy conditioned on reference path observations, trained with reward function focused on goal-reaching objectives without explicit path-following rewards.
AI.result: Significantly improves navigation efficiency with high-quality paths, maintains baseline-level performance with severely degraded or non-existent path observations.
AI.conclusion: Method is well-suited for long-range navigation where high-level plans are approximate and local execution must remain adaptive to uncertainty, through implicit conditioning that allows opportunistic path exploitation while maintaining robustness.

title: StatePlane: A Cognitive State Plane for Long-Horizon AI Systems Under Bounded Context
summary: Large language models (LLMs) and small language models (SLMs) operate under strict context window and key-value (KV) cache constraints, fundamentally limiting their ability to reason coherently over long interaction horizons. Existing approaches -- extended context windows, retrieval-augmented generation, summarization, or static documentation -- treat memory as static storage and fail to preserve decision-relevant state under long-running, multi-session tasks. We introduce StatePlane, a model-agnostic cognitive state plane that governs the formation, evolution, retrieval, and decay of episodic, semantic, and procedural state for AI systems operating under bounded context. Grounded in cognitive psychology and systems design, StatePlane formalizes episodic segmentation, selective encoding via information-theoretic constraints, goal-conditioned retrieval with intent routing, reconstructive state synthesis, and adaptive forgetting. We present a formal state model, KV-aware algorithms, security and governance mechanisms including write-path anti-poisoning, enterprise integration pathways, and an evaluation framework with six domain-specific benchmarks. StatePlane demonstrates that long-horizon intelligence can be achieved without expanding context windows or retraining models.

AI.tldr: StatePlane is a model-agnostic cognitive state plane that manages episodic, semantic, and procedural memory for AI systems under bounded context constraints, enabling long-horizon reasoning without expanding context windows or retraining models.
AI.motivation: Current LLMs/SLMs are limited by strict context window and KV cache constraints, preventing coherent reasoning over long interaction horizons. Existing memory approaches (extended context, RAG, summarization, static docs) treat memory as static storage and fail to preserve decision-relevant state in multi-session tasks.
AI.method: StatePlane formalizes episodic segmentation, selective encoding via information-theoretic constraints, goal-conditioned retrieval with intent routing, reconstructive state synthesis, and adaptive forgetting. It includes a formal state model, KV-aware algorithms, security mechanisms (write-path anti-poisoning), enterprise integration, and evaluation framework.
AI.result: StatePlane demonstrates that long-horizon intelligence can be achieved without expanding context windows or retraining models, as evaluated through six domain-specific benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: StatePlane provides a comprehensive cognitive state management system that enables AI systems to maintain and evolve decision-relevant state across long-running, multi-session tasks while operating under bounded context constraints.

title: DINOv3 with Test-Time Calibration for Automated Carotid Intima-Media Thickness Measurement on CUBS v1
summary: Carotid intima-media thickness (CIMT) measured from B-mode ultrasound is an established vascular biomarker for atherosclerosis and cardiovascular risk stratification. Although a wide range of computerized methods have been proposed for carotid boundary delineation and CIMT estimation, robust and transferable deep models that jointly address segmentation and measurement remain underexplored, particularly in the era of vision foundation models. Motivated by recent advances in adapting DINOv3 to medical segmentation and exploiting DINOv3 in test-time optimization pipelines, we investigate a DINOv3-based framework for carotid intima-media complex segmentation and subsequent CIMT measurement on the Carotid Ultrasound Boundary Study (CUBS) v1 dataset. Our pipeline predicts the intima-media band at a fixed image resolution, extracts upper and lower boundaries column-wise, corrects for image resizing using the per-image calibration factor provided by CUBS, and reports CIMT in physical units. Across three patient-level test splits, our method achieved a mean test Dice of 0.7739 $\pm$ 0.0037 and IoU of 0.6384 $\pm$ 0.0044. The mean CIMT absolute error was 181.16 $\pm$ 11.57 $μ$m, with a mean Pearson correlation of 0.480 $\pm$ 0.259. In a held-out validation subset ($n=28$), test-time threshold calibration reduced the mean absolute CIMT error from 141.0 $μ$m at the default threshold to 101.1 $μ$m at the measurement-optimized threshold, while simultaneously reducing systematic bias toward zero. Relative to the error ranges reported in the original CUBS benchmark for classical computerized methods, these results place a DINOv3-based approach within the clinically relevant $\sim$0.1 mm measurement regime. Together, our findings support the feasibility of using vision foundation models for interpretable, calibration-aware CIMT measurement.

AI.tldr: DINOv3-based framework achieves clinically relevant carotid intima-media thickness (CIMT) measurement with 0.1mm accuracy, outperforming classical methods on ultrasound segmentation.
AI.motivation: Despite many computerized methods for carotid boundary delineation and CIMT estimation, robust and transferable deep learning models that jointly address segmentation and measurement remain underexplored, especially using vision foundation models like DINOv3.
AI.method: DINOv3-based framework for carotid intima-media complex segmentation on CUBS v1 dataset, predicting intima-media band at fixed resolution, extracting boundaries column-wise, correcting for image resizing using calibration factors, and reporting CIMT in physical units with test-time threshold calibration.
AI.result: Achieved mean Dice 0.7739 ± 0.0037, IoU 0.6384 ± 0.0044, mean CIMT absolute error 181.16 ± 11.57 μm, Pearson correlation 0.480 ± 0.259. Test-time calibration reduced error from 141.0 μm to 101.1 μm while reducing systematic bias toward zero.
AI.conclusion: DINOv3-based approach achieves clinically relevant ~0.1 mm measurement accuracy, demonstrating feasibility of vision foundation models for interpretable, calibration-aware CIMT measurement in cardiovascular risk assessment.

title: Evidence-based Distributional Alignment for Large Language Models
summary:
Distributional alignment enables large language models (LLMs) to predict how a target population distributes its responses across answer options, rather than collapsing disagreement into a single consensus answer. However, existing LLM-based distribution prediction is often unstable and degrades under cultural and domain shift. Token score-based estimates can change with minor option wording or formatting, response sampling-based estimates are expensive and sensitive to prompts and decoding settings, and directly generated distributions are frequently miscalibrated.
  We propose Evi-DA, an evidence-based alignment technique that improves the fidelity and robustness of LLM-based distribution estimation under domain and cultural shift. Given a target country and a multiple-choice question, Evi-DA retrieves related World Values Survey items and their answer distributions, predicts a coarse Welzel value signature for each option, and infers the country-conditioned answer distribution in a structured format. We train the LLMs using a two-stage pipeline, where reinforcement learning optimizes survey-derived rewards that encourage accurate intermediate value predictions, faithful final distributions, well-formed structured outputs, and reduced cultural bias. Across in-domain and out-of-domain benchmarks and multiple open-source backbones, Evi-DA reduces Jensen-Shannon divergence between predicted and gold distributions relative to strong baselines, with average relative improvements of up to 44%.

AI.tldr: Evi-DA: Evidence-based distributional alignment technique that improves LLM-based distribution estimation fidelity and robustness under domain/cultural shift by using World Values Survey data and structured value prediction.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based distribution prediction methods are unstable, degrade under cultural/domain shift, sensitive to wording/formatting, expensive, and produce miscalibrated distributions.
AI.method: Two-stage pipeline: retrieves related World Values Survey items and distributions, predicts coarse Welzel value signatures for each option, infers country-conditioned answer distributions in structured format using RL with survey-derived rewards.
AI.result: Reduces Jensen-Shannon divergence between predicted and gold distributions by up to 44% relative to strong baselines across in-domain/out-of-domain benchmarks with multiple open-source backbones.
AI.conclusion: Evi-DA improves fidelity and robustness of LLM-based distribution estimation under domain and cultural shift through evidence-based alignment with structured value prediction.

authors: A. S. M. Ahsanul Sarkar Akib
title: LineMaster Pro: A Low-Cost Intelligent Line Following Robot with PID Control and Ultrasonic Obstacle Avoidance for Educational Robotics
summary: Line following robots are fundamental platforms in robotics education, yet commercially available solutions remain prohibitively expensive ($150-300$) while lacking integrated obstacle detection capabilities essential for real-world applications. This paper presents LineMaster Pro, an intelligent low-cost line following robot implemented on an Arduino Nano platform that integrates dual TCRT5000 infrared sensors for precision line tracking, an HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor for real-time obstacle detection, a digitally tuned PID controller with Ziegler-Nichols optimization, and a hierarchical finite state machine for robust obstacle avoidance. A systematic four-phase sensor calibration methodology ensures reliable operation across varying lighting and surface conditions. Experimental validation through 200 controlled trials and 72-hour continuous operation demonstrates mean tracking accuracy of 1.18 cm at 0.4 m/s (95\% CI [1.06, 1.30]), obstacle detection reliability of 96.7\% within 10-40 cm range with 0.7\% false positive rate, and 94\% successful recovery from path deviations. The PID implementation achieves 43\% improvement over conventional on-off control ($p<0.001$). At a total hardware cost of \$28.50 based on verified Bangladesh market prices, LineMaster Pro achieves a 94\% cost reduction compared to commercial alternatives, establishing a practical benchmark for accessible robotics education in resource-constrained environments.

AI.tldr: LineMaster Pro is a low-cost ($28.50) Arduino-based line following robot with integrated obstacle detection, achieving 94% cost reduction compared to commercial alternatives while maintaining high performance through PID control and systematic calibration.
AI.motivation: Commercial line following robots are expensive ($150-300) and lack integrated obstacle detection capabilities, making them inaccessible for robotics education in resource-constrained environments.
AI.method: Implemented on Arduino Nano with dual TCRT5000 infrared sensors for line tracking, HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor for obstacle detection, digitally tuned PID controller with Ziegler-Nichols optimization, hierarchical finite state machine for obstacle avoidance, and four-phase sensor calibration methodology.
AI.result: Mean tracking accuracy of 1.18 cm at 0.4 m/s, obstacle detection reliability of 96.7% within 10-40 cm range (0.7% false positive), 94% successful recovery from path deviations, 43% improvement over conventional on-off control, and 200 trials/72-hour continuous operation validation.
AI.conclusion: LineMaster Pro establishes a practical benchmark for accessible robotics education in resource-constrained environments with 94% cost reduction compared to commercial alternatives while maintaining robust performance through systematic design and calibration.

title: LLM-MINE: Large Language Model based Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias Phenotypes Mining from Clinical Notes
summary: Accurate extraction of Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias (ADRD) phenotypes from electronic health records (EHR) is critical for early-stage detection and disease staging. However, this information is usually embedded in unstructured textual data rather than tabular data, making it difficult to be extracted accurately. We therefore propose LLM-MINE, a Large Language Model-based phenotype mining framework for automatic extraction of ADRD phenotypes from clinical notes. Using two expert-defined phenotype lists, we evaluate the extracted phenotypes by examining their statistical significance across cohorts and their utility for unsupervised disease staging. Chi-square analyses confirm statistically significant phenotype differences across cohorts, with memory impairment being the strongest discriminator. Few-shot prompting with the combined phenotype lists achieves the best clustering performance (ARI=0.290, NMI=0.232), substantially outperforming biomedical NER and dictionary-based baselines. Our results demonstrate that LLM-based phenotype extraction is a promising tool for discovering clinically meaningful ADRD signals from unstructured notes.

AI.tldr: LLM-MINE framework uses large language models to extract Alzheimer's Disease phenotypes from clinical notes, outperforming traditional methods and enabling better disease staging.
AI.motivation: ADRD phenotype information is embedded in unstructured EHR text rather than tabular data, making accurate extraction difficult for early detection and disease staging.
AI.method: Proposed LLM-MINE framework using large language models for automatic phenotype extraction from clinical notes, evaluated with expert-defined phenotype lists and statistical significance testing.
AI.result: Memory impairment was the strongest discriminator; few-shot prompting with combined phenotype lists achieved best clustering performance (ARI=0.290, NMI=0.232), substantially outperforming biomedical NER and dictionary-based baselines.
AI.conclusion: LLM-based phenotype extraction is a promising tool for discovering clinically meaningful ADRD signals from unstructured clinical notes.

title: Taming Vision Priors for Data Efficient mmWave Channel Modeling
summary: Accurately modeling millimeter-wave (mmWave) propagation is essential for real-time AR and autonomous systems. Differentiable ray tracing offers a physics-grounded solution but still facing deployment challenges due to its over-reliance on exhaustive channel measurements or brittle, hand-tuned scene models for material properties. We present VisRFTwin, a scalable and data-efficient digital-twin framework that integrates vision-derived material priors with differentiable ray tracing. Multi-view images from commodity cameras are processed by a frozen Vision-Language Model to extract dense semantic embeddings, which are translated into initial estimates of permittivity and conductivity for scene surfaces. These priors initialize a Sionna-based differentiable ray tracer, which rapidly calibrates material parameters via gradient descent with only a few dozen sparse channel soundings. Once calibrated, the association between vision features and material parameters is retained, enabling fast transfer to new scenarios without repeated calibration. Evaluations across three real-world scenarios, including office interiors, urban canyons, and dynamic public spaces show that VisRFTwin reduces channel measurement needs by up to 10$\times$ while achieving a 59% lower median delay spread error than pure data-driven deep learning methods.

AI.tldr: VisRFTwin: A scalable digital-twin framework that combines vision-derived material priors with differentiable ray tracing to reduce mmWave channel measurement needs by 10× while improving accuracy over pure data-driven methods.
AI.motivation: Current differentiable ray tracing methods for mmWave propagation modeling face deployment challenges due to excessive reliance on exhaustive channel measurements or brittle hand-tuned scene models for material properties, limiting scalability and data efficiency.
AI.method: Multi-view images from commodity cameras are processed by a frozen Vision-Language Model to extract dense semantic embeddings, which are translated into initial estimates of permittivity and conductivity for scene surfaces. These priors initialize a Sionna-based differentiable ray tracer that calibrates material parameters via gradient descent using only a few dozen sparse channel soundings.
AI.result: Evaluations across three real-world scenarios (office interiors, urban canyons, dynamic public spaces) show VisRFTwin reduces channel measurement needs by up to 10× while achieving 59% lower median delay spread error than pure data-driven deep learning methods.
AI.conclusion: VisRFTwin provides a scalable, data-efficient digital-twin framework that integrates vision-derived material priors with differentiable ray tracing, enabling fast transfer to new scenarios without repeated calibration and significantly reducing deployment barriers for mmWave propagation modeling.

title: Task Expansion and Cross Refinement for Open-World Conditional Modeling
summary: Open-world conditional modeling (OCM), requires a single model to answer arbitrary conditional queries across heterogeneous datasets, where observed variables and targets vary and arise from a vast open-ended task universe. Because any finite collection of real-world datasets covers only a small fraction of this space, we propose Task Expansion and Cross Refinement (TEXR), a semi-supervised framework that enlarges effective task coverage through structured synthesis and refinement of semantic data contexts. TEXR first generates diverse uninstantiated dataset schemas and weakly instantiates them via structured probabilistic generators guided by large language models. It then performs cross-model refinement by training on disjoint data partitions and revising synthetic values across splits to reduce confirmation bias and improve pseudo-value quality. The refined synthetic datasets are aggregated with real data to train a unified conditional model. Across heterogeneous tabular benchmarks, TEXR consistently improves zero-, few-, and many-shot performance for multiple OCM backbones, demonstrating that structured task expansion and cross refinement enhance open-world conditional modeling.

AI.tldr: TEXR framework improves open-world conditional modeling by expanding task coverage through structured synthesis and cross refinement of synthetic datasets.
AI.motivation: Real-world datasets only cover a small fraction of possible conditional queries in open-world modeling, creating a coverage gap that limits model generalization across heterogeneous tasks.
AI.method: Two-stage approach: 1) Generate diverse uninstantiated dataset schemas and weakly instantiate them using structured probabilistic generators guided by LLMs; 2) Perform cross-model refinement by training on disjoint data partitions and revising synthetic values across splits to reduce confirmation bias.
AI.result: TEXR consistently improves zero-, few-, and many-shot performance across heterogeneous tabular benchmarks for multiple OCM backbones, demonstrating enhanced open-world conditional modeling.
AI.conclusion: Structured task expansion and cross refinement effectively enhance open-world conditional modeling by enlarging effective task coverage and improving synthetic data quality.

title: Data-Driven Autoregressive Power Prediction for GTernal Robots in the Robotarium
summary: Energy-aware algorithms for multi-robot systems require accurate power consumption models, yet existing approaches rely on kinematic approximations that fail to capture the complex dynamics of real hardware. We present a lightweight autoregressive predictor for the GTernal mobile robot platform deployed in the Georgia Tech Robotarium. Through analysis of 48,000 samples collected across six motion trials, we discover that power consumption exhibits strong temporal autocorrelation ($ρ_1 = 0.95$) that dominates kinematic effects. A 7,041-parameter multi-layer perceptron (MLP) achieves $R^2 = 0.90$ on held-out motion patterns by conditioning on recent power history, reaching the theoretical prediction ceiling imposed by measurement noise. Physical validation across seven robots in a collision avoidance scenario yields mean $R^2 = 0.87$, demonstrating zero-shot transfer to unseen robots and behaviors. The predictor runs in 224 $μ$s per inference, enabling real-time deployment at 150$\times$ the platform's 30 Hz control rate. We release the trained model and dataset to support energy-aware multi-robot algorithm development.

AI.tldr: Lightweight MLP predictor achieves accurate real-time power consumption forecasting for mobile robots by leveraging strong temporal autocorrelation in power data, enabling energy-aware multi-robot algorithms.
AI.motivation: Existing energy-aware algorithms for multi-robot systems rely on inaccurate kinematic approximations that fail to capture real hardware dynamics, creating a need for accurate power consumption models.
AI.method: Developed a lightweight 7,041-parameter multi-layer perceptron (MLP) that conditions on recent power history to predict future consumption. Trained on 48,000 samples from six motion trials on GTernal robots in Georgia Tech Robotarium.
AI.result: Model achieves R² = 0.90 on held-out motion patterns, reaching theoretical prediction ceiling limited by measurement noise. Physical validation across seven robots in collision avoidance yields mean R² = 0.87, demonstrating zero-shot transfer. Runs in 224 μs per inference (150× faster than 30 Hz control rate).
AI.conclusion: Power consumption in mobile robots exhibits strong temporal autocorrelation (ρ₁ = 0.95) that dominates kinematic effects. The lightweight autoregressive predictor enables real-time energy-aware algorithm deployment with released model and dataset for community use.

title: TheraAgent: Multi-Agent Framework with Self-Evolving Memory and Evidence-Calibrated Reasoning for PET Theranostics
summary: PET theranostics is transforming precision oncology, yet treatment response varies substantially; many patients receiving 177Lu-PSMA radioligand therapy (RLT) for metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) fail to respond, demanding reliable pre-therapy prediction. While LLM-based agents have shown remarkable potential in complex medical diagnosis, their application to PET theranostic outcome prediction remains unexplored, which faces three key challenges: (1) data and knowledge scarcity: RLT was only FDA-approved in 2022, yielding few training cases and insufficient domain knowledge in general LLMs; (2) heterogeneous information integration: robust prediction hinges on structured knowledge extraction from PET/CT, laboratory tests, and free-text clinical documentation; (3) evidence-grounded reasoning: clinical decisions must be anchored in trial evidence rather than LLM hallucinations. In this paper, we present TheraAgent, to our knowledge, the first agentic framework for PET theranostics, with three core innovations: (1) Multi-Expert Feature Extraction with Confidence-Weighted Consensus, where three specialized experts process heterogeneous inputs with uncertainty quantification; (2) Self-Evolving Agentic Memory (SEA-Mem), which learns prognostic patterns from accumulated cases, enabling case-based reasoning from limited data; (3) Evidence-Calibrated Reasoning, integrating a curated theranostics knowledge base to ground predictions in VISION/TheraP trial evidence. Evaluated on 35 real patients and 400 synthetic cases, TheraAgent achieves 75.7% overall accuracy on real patients and 87.0% on synthetic cases, outperforming MDAgents and MedAgent-Pro by over 20%. These results highlight a promising blueprint for trustworthy AI agents in PET theranostics, enabling trial-calibrated, multi-source decision support. Code will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: TheraAgent: First agentic framework for PET theranostics that predicts 177Lu-PSMA RLT outcomes using multi-expert feature extraction, self-evolving memory, and evidence-calibrated reasoning.
AI.motivation: Treatment response to 177Lu-PSMA radioligand therapy varies substantially, with many mCRPC patients failing to respond. LLM-based agents show potential but face challenges: data scarcity (FDA-approved 2022), heterogeneous information integration, and need for evidence-grounded reasoning.
AI.method: Three core innovations: (1) Multi-Expert Feature Extraction with Confidence-Weighted Consensus (three specialized experts with uncertainty quantification), (2) Self-Evolving Agentic Memory (SEA-Mem) for case-based reasoning from limited data, (3) Evidence-Calibrated Reasoning integrating curated theranostics knowledge base from VISION/TheraP trials.
AI.result: Achieves 75.7% overall accuracy on 35 real patients and 87.0% on 400 synthetic cases, outperforming MDAgents and MedAgent-Pro by over 20%.
AI.conclusion: TheraAgent provides a promising blueprint for trustworthy AI agents in PET theranostics, enabling trial-calibrated, multi-source decision support for predicting RLT outcomes.

title: VisualLeakBench: Auditing the Fragility of Large Vision-Language Models against PII Leakage and Social Engineering
summary: As Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) are increasingly deployed in agent-integrated workflows and other deployment-relevant settings, their robustness against semantic visual attacks remains under-evaluated -- alignment is typically tested on explicit harmful content rather than privacy-critical multimodal scenarios.
